L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: No pin check was performed prior to exit that would have detected the pull-up cord left in the closing loop. The narrative explicitly states this event 'made everyone realize even the experts required a pin check prior to launch,' indicating the absence of a gear check that accepted practice requires and that would have caught the rigging error.

L2_preconditions.label: Gusty crosswinds degraded opening heading
L2_preconditions.description: The gusty mid-day conditions with crosswinds at the exit directly degraded opening heading performance. Wind at opening altitude is explicitly identified as a factor affecting openings, and the crosswinds mechanically contributed to the 180° off-heading that resulted in object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Pauli did not have his own parachute gear and borrowed a slow-opening Unit system. This system required significantly more altitude to fully deploy than a standard BASE configuration, meaning the inadequate static line knot left zero margin for recovery from the 170m antenna.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Carl's canopy was unmodified — lacking a deep brake setting and modern tail pouch — despite a known severe surge characteristic. JD Walker offered to perform these modifications before the trip, but Carl refused. This inadequate equipment configuration directly enabled the excessive forward speed on opening that produced the fatal wall strike after the 180° off-heading.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known surge problem
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple people had jumped the Goliath canopy and observed its severe surge on opening. JD Walker specifically offered to install a deep brake setting before the Norway trip. Carl's sustained refusal to address this known, recurring equipment deficiency over time constitutes a failure to correct a known problem that directly contributed to the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first BASE jump, attempted from Trollveggen — a terminal cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking and object avoidance skills. Accepted progression norms call for beginning BASE at simple objects (spans) and building exit and deployment skills before progressing to cliffs. A first-ever BASE jump from a terminal cliff represents a clear insufficiency in BASE experience for the task attempted, directly contributing to the unstable exit and deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only five or six previous BASE jumps, which is very early in BASE progression. At this stage (1986, before dedicated BASE equipment was widely established), the jumper lacked sufficient experience to recognize that skydiving equipment was inadequate for a 500-foot slider-down antenna jump. This inexperience directly contributed to the decision to use skydiving gear with an undersized pilot chute for a low-airspeed BASE application.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Mike had only 4 BASE jumps and this was his first solo pack job. Static line rigging is a critical skill requiring proficiency; with minimal BASE experience and no prior solo packing experience, he lacked the practiced competence to execute the rigging correctly.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate gear check
L2_preconditions.description: An experienced BASE jumper checked Mike's pack job but failed to detect the static line rigging error that caused the line to part before canopy extraction. This gear check failure allowed the misrigging to go undetected.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only one previous BASE jump, which itself was unstable. With this minimal experience, he lacked the knowledge and judgment to recognize that skydiving equipment is inappropriate for slider-down cliff jumping and to properly configure gear for a 6-second delay from a cliff. This inexperience directly contributed to the inappropriate equipment selection.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only one prior BASE jump — which was itself an unstable head-down launch — the jumper had not developed the exit skills necessary for stable cliff exits. This lack of experience and training directly contributed to the unstable exit producing the off-heading opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's previous BASE jump one week earlier resulted in an unstable head-down launch from the same site. Despite this direct experience of exit instability, he returned to the same site without evidence of having corrected the problem, and the same failure mode (unstable exit) contributed to the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: When Bridge Day Jumpmasters advised Steve that his skydiving PC/bridle setup was not appropriate, he responded 'It's always worked before,' indicating he was aware others considered it deficient but chose not to correct it based on past success. This reflects a sustained pattern of using inadequate equipment despite feedback, which directly enabled the L1 decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states David misunderstood a highly experienced jumper's written advice about hand-held PC technique, exposing mesh in addition to the PC top. This knowledge gap — confusing the correct amount of PC to expose — directly produced the improper hold that caused premature partial inflation. The misunderstanding of correspondence indicates he lacked the hands-on training or mentorship that would have corrected this specific technique error.

L2_preconditions.label: Belly band tightened after gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative speculates that Robert tightened his belly band after the final gear check performed by the first jumper. This post-check modification increased pin tension on the curved-pin closure system, directly contributing to the pin lock that caused the pilot chute in tow. The gear check that had been performed could not catch a problem introduced after it was completed.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Darren had been advised multiple times to correct his head-down exits, and his previous three jumps all exhibited the same head-down exit problem. Despite this recurring issue and explicit feedback, he failed to take corrective action before attempting a more demanding object.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience for object
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the hotel was 'for the more experienced,' and Darren had only a few prior BASE jumps with a persistent unresolved exit technique problem. He went without his mentor and had not yet demonstrated basic exit proficiency, indicating insufficient experience for this building jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment for the jump
L2_preconditions.description: Darren used a modified skydiving canopy (Pursuit 230) in a borrowed skydiving rig rather than purpose-built BASE equipment. A skydiving rig and modified canopy may have contributed to the severity of the surge and opening characteristics following the off-heading deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Jonathan used a brand-new container system with a BOC pouch that was reportedly too large for his 38" pilot chute, and he had never used this BOC style before. The pouch-to-PC size mismatch created the condition for the PC to inadvertently extract during freefall. Additionally, he pulled fabric out of the pouch during pin checks to have 'plenty to grab,' further compromising the stowage security. This inadequate equipment configuration directly enabled the premature deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Unmodified canopy for slider-down
L2_preconditions.description: The PD 230 was a brand-new, unmodified skydiving canopy jumped without the standard BASE modifications discussed (deep brake setting, tail pouch with locking stow, relocated steering lines). Without a deep brake setting, the canopy surged violently upon premature opening, directly contributing to the collision and fatal outcome. While the premature deployment was the primary failure, the unmodified canopy's violent surge behavior amplified the consequences.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Joe was so nervous about the jump that friends later remarked about it. This level of nervousness, notable enough for others to comment on, suggests the jumper may have been exceeding their readiness for this specific jump, and proceeding despite this state likely degraded exit and response performance.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Joe 'failed to adequately respond' to the off-heading opening, suggesting a possible freeze or inadequate reaction under the stress of an unexpected 180° off-heading facing the wall. His notably high pre-jump nervousness may have compounded this inability to execute a timely correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states this fatality 'began the process of insisting people who begin BASE jumping have at least 150 previous parachute jumps,' and references 'people without much experience being handed BASE jumps beyond their abilities.' This directly indicates the jumper lacked sufficient skydiving experience to manage the freefall body position and altitude awareness demands of a terminal slider-up cliff jump from El Capitan.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly identifies the issue of 'people without much experience being handed BASE jumps beyond their abilities.' El Capitan is a terminal slider-up cliff requiring competent sub-terminal/terminal tracking, altitude awareness, and body position management. The jumper's inability to maintain proper body position and altitude awareness during a 15-second delay indicates experience insufficient for this object.

L2_preconditions.label: Loss of Altitude Awareness
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'lost altitude awareness' while holding a head-high attitude. This failure to maintain situational awareness of proximity to the wall during freefall directly enabled the skill-based error of backing into the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper held only an expired USPA A license, the lowest skydiving certification, indicating minimal skydiving experience far below the accepted 200+ skydive minimum for beginning BASE. This insufficient foundation meant the jumper lacked the knowledge to understand why skydiving equipment is inappropriate for BASE jumping and why BASE-specific training is required.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states there is nothing to suggest any previous BASE experience or training. The jumper attempted a BASE jump without a First Jump Course, mentor guidance, or any BASE-specific education, which would have taught that skydiving gear cannot be used for BASE and that BASE requires specialized equipment, packing, and deployment configurations.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was an experienced skydiver who came along as ground crew and only decided to make a BASE jump later in the week. There is no indication of prior BASE experience, a first jump course, or mentorship. This lack of BASE-specific training and experience directly contributed to the inappropriate equipment and technique selection—an unmodified square canopy with no DBS on a sheer cliff requiring freefall delay and a round canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper used an unmodified square canopy with no deep brake setting or other modifications appropriate for a slider-down cliff jump. Proper pre-jump preparation requires configuring the canopy with DBS to minimize forward speed on opening near a sheer wall. This configuration failure directly enabled the object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Don had only 30 skydives, far below the accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE. This grossly insufficient skydiving experience means he lacked the foundational canopy piloting skills needed to manage a tight landing area, directly contributing to the panic hook turn into the canyon wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Don was 'an idiot when it came to assessing risks,' supported by a pattern of behavior including forging a logbook for an untrained friend, taking that friend on a two-way skydive that 'almost killed them both,' and subsequently taking the same friend off a tower. This pattern of grossly overestimating his own capability and disregarding risk directly contributed to attempting a jump at a site with a tight landing area that exceeded his canopy piloting ability.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Sébastien was tracking on his back to film the second jumper and focused on closing the gap between them for video purposes. This channelized attention on the filming task diverted his awareness from monitoring object separation and terrain proximity, directly contributing to insufficient separation from the wall and late recognition of the need to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Stability
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper spent 12 seconds attempting to recover stability before deploying, indicating channelized attention on regaining a stable body position to the exclusion of altitude awareness. This fixation on stability recovery directly delayed the deployment that produced the fatal low pull.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports the jumper was offered instruction prior to the jump and refused it. The failure to deploy despite prolonged instability — prioritizing stability over pulling — reflects a knowledge gap regarding the fundamental BASE principle that deployment must occur regardless of body position when altitude is critical. Accepted BASE training explicitly teaches that deploying unstable is preferable to impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known brake setting deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been repeatedly advised by other jumpers to apply deeper brake settings to his non-BASE specific canopy to avoid its excessive surge and drive. This represents a sustained pattern of failing to correct a known equipment configuration problem despite repeated feedback from peers.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known canopy response deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been repeatedly told by other jumpers to be more responsive during the canopy opening sequence. This indicates a recurring performance issue that the jumper was aware of through direct feedback but failed to correct over time, directly contributing to the failure to execute evasive maneuvers.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had no prior BASE training or experience. Accepted BASE education covers the critical differences between skydiving and BASE equipment, including the unsuitability of collapsible pilot chutes at sub-terminal airspeeds. This knowledge gap directly enabled the decision to use an unmodified skydiving rig.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states alcohol may have been a contributing factor. While qualified as possible, this is explicitly noted in the report as a potential contributor to the jumper's impaired judgment in deciding to use skydiving gear for a BASE jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Obscured visibility from fog
L2_preconditions.description: Low level clouds/fog bank eliminated visual references during canopy flight near the cliff face. This directly impaired the jumper's ability to perceive terrain proximity and maintain object separation, resulting in object strike under canopy.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper instinctively reached for the BOC position she was accustomed to, indicating her habitual motor pattern dominated her attention. This channelized focus on the familiar BOC location prevented her from redirecting to the actual leg-mounted pouch location before running out of altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment familiarization
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper switched to a system with an older-style leg-mounted pilot chute pouch that she was not accustomed to jumping, having habitually used a BOC pouch system. Adequate pre-jump preparation for an unfamiliar deployment system would include practice touches and deliberate rehearsal of the different pull location to override habitual motor patterns.

L2_preconditions.label: Improper slider configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The updated narrative indicates Roger was packed slider-up after jumping the higher Perrine Bridge all weekend, and it is unknown whether staying slider-up for the significantly lower Hansen Bridge (350 ft) was intentional. Slider-up configuration on a 350 ft bridge would produce a slower, higher opening consuming more altitude, directly reducing canopy flight time and glide available to reach the landing zone — contributing to his inability to make the planned LZ and landing in moving water instead.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that Trevor 'was caught in a time warp as far as BASE equipment is concerned,' indicating he lacked current knowledge of proper BASE equipment requirements. His use of a skydiving container with a grossly undersized PC, short bridle, deployment bag, and sail slider reflects a fundamental knowledge gap about BASE-specific equipment configuration that accepted BASE education would have corrected.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Andrea was filming two other jumpers during freefall, which diverted his attention from monitoring altitude. The narrative explicitly identifies 'focusing on filming' as a possible factor and notes this is the second BASE fatality involving flying back-to-earth with a camera, indicating the filming task channelized his attention away from altitude awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Andrea was flying in a back-to-earth position to film the other jumpers. This orientation deprived him of visual ground references needed to assess altitude, contributing to vestibular and visual spatial disorientation that directly enabled the over-delay.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Terry had only 8 BASE jumps total and was attempting a terminal slider-up cliff jump requiring sub-terminal tracking and reliable deployment technique. Accepted progression norms require extensive experience at simpler objects before progressing to slider-up cliffs, and video evidence confirms his tracking and directional control skills were inadequate for this object. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was offered instruction prior to the jump and refused it. This indicates the jumper lacked the training and mentorship that accepted practice requires, which directly contributed to the inability to recover from an unstable exit or deploy the parachute during freefall.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper refused offered instruction before attempting a terminal slider-up cliff jump. Refusing instruction for a task that clearly exceeded the jumper's demonstrated capability (as evidenced by the inability to recover from instability or deploy) indicates the jumper unreasonably overestimated their own ability to manage the jump without guidance.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, and no experienced jumper present. Without any skydiving or BASE training, he lacked the fundamental skills for exit technique, body position control, and object separation, directly contributing to the unstable exit and cliff strike.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had zero BASE jumps, no first jump course, no mentor, and no experienced companion. This complete absence of training and education meant he lacked the knowledge framework to recognize that attempting a BASE jump without foundational skills was a fatal-level decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check after conversion
L2_preconditions.description: After converting from slider-down to slider-up configuration at the last minute, the jumper did not perform a gear check that would have caught the misrouted steering lines. A systematic inspection of the rigging after the conversion — tracing brake lines through slider grommets as accepted practice requires — would have detected the omission before the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses attested that Thierry was 'really very tired' after completing ten jumps in the valley over the two days prior to his fatal jump. This fatigue likely degraded his judgment when deciding to perform an aerobatic back-to-earth exit from a cliff with limited vertical relief.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: Thierry had only ~100 skydives, which is below the accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE jumping. With 240 BASE jumps but a limited skydiving foundation, his core freefall body control skills — particularly recovery from non-standard orientations — may have been insufficient, directly contributing to his slow recovery from the back-to-earth exit position.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 3 total BASE jumps and was attempting a slider-up cliff jump. Accepted progression norms establish that slider-up cliffs require sub-terminal tracking skill, refined exit technique, and object avoidance skills developed over many jumps at simpler objects. With only 2-3 BASE jumps, she lacked the experience necessary to execute a stable exit with adequate separation from a cliff object.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: It had been raining and the wooden launch ramp was wet. The wet surface directly caused the jumper to slip during his exit run, producing the unstable freefall and back-to-earth deployment that led to the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience for cliff
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first cliff jump after only 15 tower jumps (16 total BASE jumps) with no reported prior cliff experience. Accepted progression norms require developing object avoidance skills at spans before progressing to cliffs, and sub-terminal tracking is mandatory for slider-up cliffs. The jumper's limited experience base left him without the skills needed to recover from the unstable exit on a 700-foot cliff with a mid-face outcropping.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE jump (this fatal jump was their first), no reported skydiving experience, and no evidence of a first jump course or mentorship. This lack of BASE-specific training and knowledge directly contributed to the inappropriate gear selection, as accepted BASE education would have corrected the decision to use a modified skydiving rig with a canopy known to be unsuitable for BASE.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 2 prior BASE jumps and approximately 200 skydives. With minimal BASE experience, the jumper lacked the practiced exit skills needed for a slider-down cliff jump, where a stable exit is critical and there is virtually no margin for recovery from an unstable body position. This level of experience is clearly insufficient for the task attempted, directly contributing to the failed exit execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Lee was filming the other jumpers while on his back, channelizing his attention on the camera task to the exclusion of altitude/time awareness. The narrative explicitly identifies 'focusing on filming' as a possible factor, and describes him continuing to film on his back for 3 full seconds beyond the 8-second deployment point before transitioning to face-to-earth.

L2_preconditions.label: Fog Obscuring Altitude Cues
L2_preconditions.description: A layer of fog approximately 300 feet off the ground eliminated visual altitude references. The fog degraded Lee's ability to perceive his proximity to the ground, directly impairing the altitude awareness needed to execute a timely deployment. The second jumper believed Lee was expecting to come out of the fog before deploying, suggesting the fog actively removed the visual cues he relied upon.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 12 BASE jumps and was attempting a stowed deployment for the very first time. Stowed deployment requires practiced technique to cleanly extract and throw the PC from the BOC into clean air, a skill distinct from hand-held deployment. With no prior stowed BASE deployments, the jumper lacked the trained proficiency needed to execute this critical task reliably, directly contributing to the failed PC deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration for task
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper used a 38-inch PC with a 6'9" bridle on a stowed 1.5-second slider-down deployment from a span. Per accepted practice, a 46-inch PC is standard for 0-1 second slider-down delays, and 42-46 inches for ~2-second delays. A 38-inch PC is undersized for this low-airspeed application, producing less drag force and requiring more time to inflate and extract the canopy. Combined with a shorter-than-standard bridle (6'9" vs. standard 9'), the PC had less distance to clear the burble. This configuration was inadequate for the planned jump and directly contributed to the PC-in-tow malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Lori had only five previous Perrine bridge jumps before attempting her first cliff jump. Slider-up cliff jumping requires sub-terminal tracking skills and the ability to recover from unstable exits near terrain — capabilities that develop over substantially more BASE experience than five bridge jumps. This insufficient cliff-specific experience directly contributed to her inability to recover stability and achieve separation after losing control on exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that most of Nik's previous BASE jumps had been low objects and that he was actively working on his tracking skills. This indicates his tracking proficiency was still developing and insufficient for the demands of a 9-10 second terminal cliff delay requiring effective sub-terminal/terminal tracking for object separation. This training gap directly contributed to both the insufficient separation and the unstable exit/premature deployment on a long-delay cliff jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Wolfgang was focused on filming his partner's opening, which diverted his attention from tracking performance and altitude awareness. The partner's account confirms that the filming task degraded his tracking ('doing camera = bad (not perfect) tracking') and that attempting to film a 5-second delay opening at Staubbach was not feasible, indicating his attention was channelized on the camera task at the expense of monitoring his own deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate jump plan
L2_preconditions.description: The jump partner explicitly states the cause was 'stupid plan / not thinking' and that filming a 5-second delay including opening at Staubbach was not possible. The plan required the cameraman to track while filming, then deploy with adequate altitude, but the task was incompatible with the available altitude. Both jumpers had enough experience to recognize this beforehand but failed to identify the plan as unworkable during pre-jump planning.

L2_preconditions.label: Unfamiliar camera equipment
L2_preconditions.description: Wolfgang was jumping a borrowed TV station camera system (bicycle helmet with front-mounted camera and cable to belly recorder) instead of his own superior helmet setup. The partner noted the system looked 'a little bit dangerous' and was not very stable on the head. This unfamiliar, suboptimal equipment configuration may have further degraded his ability to manage the filming task efficiently while maintaining altitude awareness and tracking performance.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 16 BASE jumps, the jumper attempted a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking and a stable exit from a sketchy launch point. This is well below the experience level accepted practice requires for terminal/sub-terminal slider-up cliff objects, where exit technique, sub-terminal tracking, and recovery from poor exits are critical skills developed over many more jumps. This inexperience directly contributed to the inability to execute a stable exit and recover control.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Kirill had only 2 wingsuit BASE jumps and 20 wingsuit skydives. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps before attempting wingsuit BASE. With such minimal wingsuit BASE experience, the altitude awareness and deployment timing skills required for wingsuit terrain flight were underdeveloped, directly contributing to the fatally late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Rob was repeatedly told by his jumping partner and other experienced wingsuit pilots that the jump was not achievable, yet he remained convinced he could make it, including adding a reverse gainer. His belief that he could 'out fly the ledge right up until he died' despite unanimous expert assessment to the contrary demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability relative to the specific task, directly driving the decision to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction during rigging
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Bill was speculated to have been distracted while putting his rig together, interrupting the bridle attachment procedure. This distraction caused him to leave the lark's head knot incomplete without restarting the procedure upon return, directly enabling the incomplete bridle attachment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check tracing the deployment chain from pilot chute through bridle to canopy would have revealed that the bridle was not secured to the canopy with a completed lark's head knot. The bar tack catching in the ring gave the appearance of a correct attachment, but a hands-on inspection of the connection point would have detected the incomplete attachment.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswinds degraded separation
L2_preconditions.description: Medium to strong winds with crosswinds at exit actively degraded the jumper's ability to achieve adequate object separation during the 5-6 second delay. Locals specifically cited the high winds as a contributing factor, indicating the wind conditions mechanically reduced the effectiveness of whatever tracking and separation the jumper achieved, compounding the insufficient delay.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Highway patrol was actively attempting to arrest the jumpers, creating acute pressure to jump immediately rather than abort. This law enforcement pressure drove the decision to jump with a compromised PC rather than accept being caught, which would have been the safe alternative.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was running toward the exit point to escape highway patrol, and this haste directly caused him to drop the hand-held pilot chute. The rushing was driven by the pressure of imminent arrest rather than normal jump preparation.

L2_preconditions.label: Night conditions degraded handling
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred at night, which would have degraded the jumper's ability to securely handle the pilot chute while running to the exit point and to visually assess the entanglement after dropping and picking up the PC.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 30 BASE jumps and this being only his 2nd jump from the Nose (a slider-up cliff), the jumper had very limited experience for terminal cliff jumping. The narrative explicitly states this was his first off-heading opening, meaning he had never practiced the critical skill of instant heading correction in a real BASE scenario. Accepted progression norms emphasize extensive practice of off-heading corrections at simple spans before progressing to cliffs, precisely because the muscle memory must be automatic when facing the wall. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task of recovering from an off-heading at a cliff with limited separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Jumping Above Recognized Limits
L2_preconditions.description: Fellow jumpers reported that the jumper was very nervous at exit points on numerous occasions and may have been jumping above his limits. Persistent nervousness recognized by peers suggests the jumper was repeatedly operating beyond his comfort and capability level. This pattern of proceeding despite signals of unreadiness placed him in situations where he lacked the composure and practiced capability to handle emergencies such as the 180-degree off-heading that killed him.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states speculation that the lack of normal groundrush — unlike what the jumper was accustomed to without a wingsuit — may have fooled him into misjudging his altitude. Wingsuit flight produces a fundamentally different visual speed picture (higher horizontal speed, lower vertical speed) than non-wingsuit freefall, creating a visual illusion that degraded his ability to perceive true altitude and proximity to the ground. This perceptual failure directly explains why he held his hand on the PC handle without extracting it.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first wingsuit BASE jump and his first jump ever with a leg-mounted pilot chute pouch. He had only 50 wingsuit skydives (below the accepted minimum of ~100) and 2 balloon wingsuit jumps. The combination of a novel deployment system, unfamiliar altitude perception in wingsuit flight near terrain, and insufficient wingsuit experience directly contributed to his failure to execute a timely deployment despite having located the handle.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level clouds and foggy conditions degraded the jumper's ability to visually assess altitude and proximity to the ground. The narrative explicitly notes this is 'the third BASE fatality that is fog related,' indicating fog directly impaired altitude perception, contributing to the late pilot chute extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind degraded canopy performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports medium to strong winds with crosswinds at the exit. The press translation states a squall of local wind caused loss of canopy control. This wind condition actively degraded the jumper's ability to maintain heading and fly away from the wall after the already-late, off-heading opening that resulted from the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 11 wingsuit BASE jumps, far below the accepted minimum of 100 BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE. This insufficient experience base directly contributed to the decision to fly an unfamiliar, higher-performance wingsuit in adverse wind conditions — a decision a more experienced wingsuit BASE jumper would likely not have made.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind destabilized exit/flight
L2_preconditions.description: Medium-to-strong crosswinds reportedly contributed to the jumper's stability problems during flight. This wind-induced instability directly degraded his ability to maintain a stable body position and locate/extract the pilot chute from the leg pouch, contributing to the failure to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation from Instability
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports the jumper had stability problems, likely from the combination of an unfamiliar suit and crosswinds. An unstable or tumbling body position in a wingsuit would degrade spatial orientation and the ability to execute the practiced BOC reach motion, directly contributing to the failure to locate and extract the pilot chute.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's exceptionally effective tracking suit performance along the talus created an unusual visual environment where normal ground rush cues were absent. The narrative explicitly states he 'probably didn't experience normal ground rush as his track was really going at that point' and 'didn't notice just exactly how low he was.' This visual illusion caused him to misjudge his altitude, leaving him with thinner margins when the instability and delayed deployment occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Gear check omission
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to perform a final gear check that would have detected the pull-up cord still attached to the pilot chute. A pre-jump equipment inspection is accepted practice and would have caught this rigging error before exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only one previous BASE jump (two total including the fatal jump). Slider-up cliff jumping requires sub-terminal tracking ability, refined exit technique, and practiced object avoidance skills that are developed over many jumps at progressively more demanding objects. With only one prior BASE jump, the jumper lacked the experience to execute a stable exit and deployment from a slider-up cliff object, directly contributing to the over-rotation and unstable body position at deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failed pre-jump bridle trace
L2_preconditions.description: After turning to face away from the tower, Mario did not notice the bridle wrapped around his lower body prior to launching. Accepted practice for static line jumps requires tracing the bridle by hand from shrivel flap to anchor before jumping, and having a knowledgeable person verify the jumper is clear of rigging. Mario was alone on the tower and failed to perform this check, which would have detected the entanglement caused by the wrong-direction turn.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation from Tumble
L2_preconditions.description: The over-rotation sent the jumper fully head-down, and he spent almost the entire freefall trying to correct. The disorienting head-down attitude from the failed aerial likely degraded his ability to perceive altitude and orientation, contributing to the inability to recover and the extremely late deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Aerial Dependency
L2_preconditions.description: The companion jumper's account reveals that gainers had become a 'crutch move' used to manage exit anxiety, and that both jumpers had developed a pattern of defaulting to aerials rather than maintaining flat-exit proficiency. This normalized reliance on aerials without addressing the underlying skill deficiency contributed to attempting the front loop from this object.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: Brian had not maintained his BASE jumping or skydiving in the decades since his 1966 El Capitan jump. Despite receiving refresher training prior to Bridge Day 2006, the narrative explicitly states he had not kept up either discipline. This extended absence from active jumping left him without the practiced motor skills and muscle memory needed for a clean PC deployment under real conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: Video shows Brian performed an unintentional backflip after exit, requiring him to recover stability before deploying. Managing recovery from an unexpected unstable exit on a slider-up span jump likely consumed cognitive and physical capacity, contributing to the poor PC deployment technique (deploying into his chest rather than into clean air to the side).

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: Edgar was focused on filming the other jumper during freefall, which diverted his attention from altitude awareness, body position management, and timely deployment. The narrative explicitly lists 'Focusing on filming' as a possible factor, and the domain reference identifies back-to-earth exits as producing loss of altitude awareness — a problem compounded by the cognitive demands of operating a camera during the critical freefall phase.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Exiting on his back and attempting to transition to face-to-earth while very unstable would have degraded Edgar's ability to perceive his orientation, altitude, and proximity to the wall. The domain reference identifies loss of altitude awareness as a known serious failure mode of back-to-earth exits, and the witness described the transition as very unstable, consistent with vestibular disorientation contributing to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate Equipment Configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The witness noted Edgar was not wearing a jumpsuit or tracking clothes. For a slider-up cliff jump requiring tracking for object separation, the absence of tracking-appropriate clothing reduced his ability to generate separation from the wall after recovering from the back-to-earth position, directly contributing to insufficient clearance at deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Extreme wind degraded PC deployment
L2_preconditions.description: The 32-39 mph winds with extreme turbulence directly degraded the jumper's ability to execute a clean pilot chute deployment. The high tailwind blew the bridle back under the arm/body, contributing to the entanglement. While the decision to jump in these winds is coded at L1, the wind also mechanically impaired the deployment execution itself.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy had approximately 45 wingsuit BASE jumps and 700 skydives. The domain reference specifies a minimum of 100 wingsuit skydives (including 40-60 in the specific suit) and 100 BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump, plus extensive progression requirements. With 45 WS BASE jumps, he was still in early wingsuit BASE progression. The narrative describes classic early-progression wingsuit errors—exiting too head-high and flying in a near-stall configuration for 7 seconds—which the domain reference identifies as common mistakes stemming from insufficient skill development. This experience level directly contributed to the poor exit and inefficient flight execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy flew for approximately 22 seconds just above the snow in the couloir at an altitude too low to pull safely, yet continued flying and only deployed after 20 seconds of terrain-matching flight. This sustained flight in a no-pull zone without earlier deployment is consistent with the jumper failing to accurately perceive his altitude and proximity to terrain relative to minimum deployment altitude. The snow-covered couloir environment may have contributed to visual misperception of terrain proximity, impairing his ability to recognize he was already below survivable deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient cliff experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 3 prior cliff jumps, with the remaining 62 BASE jumps from 60-80 meter antennas. Antenna jumps of that height provide no sub-terminal tracking practice, no experience managing object separation from terrain, and no experience with the exit-to-track skills required for slider-down cliff jumping. This insufficient cliff-specific experience directly contributed to the failure to achieve adequate forward separation and the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste after long weather hold
L2_preconditions.description: The group waited over 3 hours due to very strong headwinds before jumping in conditions described as only 'marginally ok.' The prolonged weather hold created psychological pressure to jump when conditions finally improved slightly rather than continuing to wait or walking down, contributing to the decision to jump in marginal conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: No helmet worn
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper deliberately chose not to wear a helmet, which the narrative notes could have given him a chance to remain conscious and fight to turn the canopy after the first wall strike. While not producing the object strike, the absence of head protection removed a layer of survivability that could have mitigated the fatal outcome after the off-heading opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify anchor knot
L2_preconditions.description: Marcelo failed to adequately check his static line anchor knot before jumping. A pre-jump verification by tracing the rigging from container to anchor — accepted practice for static line jumps — would have detected an insecure knot. This gear check omission enabled the L1 execution failure to go undetected.

L2_preconditions.label: Pilot chute removal
L2_preconditions.description: Marcelo chose to remove his pilot chute for the static line jump. While the domain reference notes the PC should never be stowed during static line, removal eliminated the backup means of canopy extraction. Had the PC been retained, it could potentially have inflated and extracted the canopy after the anchor failed, providing a possible recovery path from the knot failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was filming other jumpers in a 3-way wingsuit formation. A fellow jumper from the load speculated that he lost attention to altitude because he was watching the others, and the video evidence supports this. Focusing on filming diverted attention from altitude awareness and timely deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper as 'very nervous' at the exit point and unable to get his footing in the right position. The witness states the exit is 'a big mind fuck' that shook even a 1700+ jump veteran. The jumper's nervousness and psychological stress at the exit point degraded his execution of the technical exit, contributing to the unstable departure.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The witness assessed that the jumper 'possibly panicked because of the mind fuck related to the jump' and that the wingsuit trapped his arms, preventing recovery. The combination of an immediately unstable exit from a psychologically intimidating, underhung exit point likely triggered a panic/freeze response that prevented the jumper from initiating deployment throughout the entire freefall, consistent with the pilot chute reportedly still being in its pouch at impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states this was the jumper's first rappel-access exit, the exit is described as technical and suited for experienced jumpers only, and the witness concludes the death resulted from the jumper pushing forward too soon relative to his experience level. The exit required a hard push to clear underhung terrain in a wingsuit — a task demanding refined wingsuit exit skills that the jumper evidently lacked, as demonstrated by the head-down, shoulder-dropped departure.

L2_preconditions.label: Difficult exit surface conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The exit point is described as having poor footing ('a bit of a shitty exit point for your feet') with grass on a slope, and the jumper could not get his footing in the right position. These exit surface conditions directly degraded his ability to execute the powerful, balanced push-off required to clear the underhung terrain, contributing to the unstable departure.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong winds degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: Strong winds were reported at the exit point. Wind at exit causes discomfort and potential instability, and for a wingsuit exit from an extended deck, strong wind could directly degrade the jumper's ability to execute a stable exit, contributing to the unstable departure described in the narrative.

L2_preconditions.label: Gusting crosswind degraded deployment
L2_preconditions.description: Gusting winds of 18-22 mph changing from crosswind to tailwind physically blew the already-inflated hand-held PC behind the jumper after the throw, preventing effective deployment. The wind directly overpowered the jumper's ability to execute a clean PC toss, causing the PC to be entrained behind the jumper rather than reaching clean air.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'remained focused on releasing them by reaching down to that left binding' and was 'only concerned with reaching that heel piece.' This channelized attention on the ski release task came at the exclusion of altitude awareness, directly contributing to the delayed deployment that was the L1 error.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Mike had approximately 8 wingsuit BASE jumps. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 WS BASE jumps before proximity flying, and Bispen is described as suitable only for very experienced wingsuit pilots. This massive experience deficit directly enabled his decision to attempt a line far beyond his capability.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit Skill
L2_preconditions.description: With only ~8 WS BASE jumps, Mike had not developed the skills to manage airspeed and angle of attack near terrain. The witness observation of slow flight close to rock is consistent with the recognized slow-flight failure mode that results from insufficient training in speed management — a skill the domain reference states requires deliberate training with significant flight time.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was Leroy's first wingsuit BASE jump. The West wall at Notch Peak is described as a technical wingsuit jump requiring proximity flying skills. A first-time wingsuit BASE jumper lacks the experience to assess whether a 600ft-to-talus technical exit is survivable, and lacks the flight skills (start arc management, speed control) needed to execute such a jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Leroy disregarded locals' recommendation not to go at all due to conditions, separated from his partner who turned back, blazed his own trail against his friend's judgment, and then chose to jump a technical exit on his first wingsuit BASE jump. This pattern of repeatedly overriding others' caution and proceeding alone demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability relative to the specific task.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site reconnaissance
L2_preconditions.description: Leroy failed to adequately identify the correct exit point. The South wall was the only exit with sufficient altitude for a non-technical wingsuit jump, but he was unable to find it and instead jumped the West wall with only 600ft to talus. Proper site preparation—obtaining detailed exit point information from locals or experienced jumpers familiar with Notch Peak—would have identified the appropriate exit or revealed that the West wall was unsuitable.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states there was 'no attempt to correct the heading' despite 3-4 seconds of canopy flight — sufficient time for at least an initial correction attempt. Combined with the observation that Scott 'looked exceptionally nervous and made several countdowns before actually jumping,' the evidence supports a freeze or startle response upon experiencing the off-heading opening with line twists, resulting in temporary inability to act on trained corrections.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Scott appeared exceptionally nervous and made several countdowns before jumping, indicating he was at or beyond his comfort level. Despite these clear signs of stress, he proceeded to jump as the third person in a group, suggesting social or self-imposed pressure to continue rather than declining. This state contributed to the weak exit that produced poor separation from the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the Mighty Penis / Via Ferrata exit is a 'Wingsuit only jump' for 'experienced Wingsuiter only,' yet the jumper attempted it with a tracking suit. This indicates the jumper either did not know or did not understand the site-specific requirement for wingsuit-level glide performance to clear the terrain, a knowledge gap that site-specific briefing or mentorship from local experienced jumpers would have corrected.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE and WS Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Geo's close friend explicitly described him as a 'beginner pilot' with only 1-2 years in the entire sport from first BASE jump to flying big wingsuits, calling him 'a classic example of too much too soon.' Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE, with extensive further progression before proximity flying. His poor exits ('classic head high student exits') confirm insufficient skill development for the terrain-clearance proximity line he attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Geo was described as confident he could clear the saddle by 50-100m, yet his close friend stated he was a beginner pilot whose actual ability was far below what the line demanded. His friend noted he was 'hanging around all the experienced jumpers' and that 'it is easy now for beginners to successfully do a basejump/wingsuit flight and think that just because it was an advanced spot then they are good.' This demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability relative to the specific task, well beyond what a reasonable jumper at his level would have assessed.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The rig was not custom-made for the jumper's body dimensions. The narrative states his larger girth caused the container to shift upward on his back when in freefall position, moving the BOC out of reach. This was his first stowed deployment from this rig after two successful hand-held jumps, indicating the BOC accessibility had not been verified in a freefall-representative body position with this specific rig.

L2_preconditions.label: Anthropometric/Biomechanical Limitations
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper as 'a bit rounder than average with big solid shoulders' and 'not the most flexible of blokes.' His body dimensions and limited flexibility directly impaired his ability to reach the BOC once the rig shifted upward in freefall position, contributing to the failed extraction across five attempts.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly describes the jumper as 'an inexperienced wingsuit pilot.' Wingsuit BASE requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps per accepted progression norms, and technical sites with mandatory performance requirements demand commensurate experience. This insufficient experience directly contributed to the inappropriate object selection.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS exit skill
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's inexperience as a wingsuit pilot directly contributed to the unstable (sideways) exit. Accepted practice requires exits that blend seamlessly into flight, a skill developed through extensive wingsuit skydiving and BASE progression. An experienced wingsuit pilot would have had the trained motor patterns to produce a stable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/group influence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'the group decided to go to a somewhat technical WS jump,' indicating the object selection was a group decision rather than the inexperienced jumper's independent choice. This group dynamic contributed to the jumper proceeding to an object inappropriate for his experience level.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to verify that the bridle was clear of his body before exiting. Accepted practice for static line jumps requires that every jumper have a knowledgeable person trace the bridle by hand from shrivel flap to anchor and verify the jumper is clear of rigging before jumping. This check would have detected the bridle wrapped around his lower extremity.

L2_preconditions.label: Unstable exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the exit area as having an 'unstable slope with disintegrated rocks,' which may have degraded the jumper's ability to execute the forceful launch required to clear the ledge 60-80m below. This exit surface condition could have directly impaired footing and push strength during the exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Herbert had only 19 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The narrative explicitly states he 'still had slightly unstable exits,' indicating he had not yet developed the exit stability and sub-terminal tracking skills required for slider-up cliff jumping. His insufficient experience directly contributed to the unstable exit and inadequate separation that produced the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: No helmet worn
L2_preconditions.description: Herbert was not wearing a helmet. While this did not cause the unstable exit, accepted practice requires a helmet on every jump. The medic stated he might have survived with a helmet. The absence of this basic protective equipment converted a survivable object strike into a fatal one.

L2_preconditions.label: Negative habit transfer
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper switched from his Vampire 3 (leg pouch deployment) to his Phantom Edge (BOC deployment) mid-session after the V3 became unusable. The ingrained muscle memory from habitual V3 use caused him to automatically reach for the wrong deployment location, a classic negative habit transfer that directly produced the delayed PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Helmet camera snag hazard
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had a GoPro camera mounted on her helmet with a clip that had previously broken and was re-attached with glue. This created a protrusion on the helmet that the bridle could catch on during deployment. Accepted practice recognizes helmet-mounted cameras as physical snag hazards; the broken and glued mount further increased entanglement risk. This equipment configuration directly enabled the bridle wrap that resulted from the weak PC toss.

L2_preconditions.label: Failed to fix broken camera mount
L2_preconditions.description: The camera clip had broken before and the jumper was seen gluing it back on before the jump rather than removing the snag hazard or properly repairing it. This indicates awareness of a known equipment deficiency that was not adequately corrected, maintaining the entanglement risk that contributed to the bridle wrapping around the camera.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience at Site
L2_preconditions.description: This was Gary's first wingsuit jump from the High Ultimate exit, a site where locals were divided on whether it was safe for wingsuit use. While he had ~400 BASE jumps and was current on his V1 wingsuit, the specific demands of a wingsuit exit from this platform—requiring precise body attitude management to clear a known ledge/outcrop—represented a task he had not previously performed. His inability to maintain the correct head-high exit attitude and transition into flight suggests the specific skill required for this exit exceeded what his experience at this particular site could support.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was only the jumper's second BASE jump at this cliff site, with the first jump completed the previous day. The narrative describes instability, poor tracking, and late deployment — a cluster of fundamental execution failures (exit-to-track transition, sub-terminal tracking, deployment timing) that indicates insufficient experience for a slider-up cliff requiring mandatory tracking and adequate object separation. Accepted progression norms require extensive practice of these skills at forgiving objects before attempting terminal/sub-terminal cliffs.

L2_preconditions.label: Cold-impaired hand function
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated before the jump that his fingers were cold. Cold fingers directly degrade fine motor function and tactile sensitivity, impairing the ability to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket, which the narrative identifies as the mechanism of the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 50 wingsuit BASE jumps across 2 BASE seasons, and having progressed through two wingsuits (Prodigy then Phantom/Firebird) in that time, the jumper lacked the experience base to accurately evaluate whether a previously unscouted, highly technical exit with only 200m of rock drop was within his capabilities for wingsuit flight. The update explicitly frames this as pushing progression too fast to technical sites.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking/object experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that the High Nose exit 'is classed as a wingsuit and experienced trackers only,' yet the jumper 'just did not get her track on.' This indicates the jumper's tracking proficiency was insufficient for the specific demands of this exit, which required experienced-level tracking skills to achieve adequate separation from the cliff's ledge system.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was Antoine's 3rd wingsuit BASE jump. The domain reference establishes that wingsuit BASE requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps before a first WS BASE jump, with extensive further progression before attempting technical lines. A 3-jump WS BASE experience level is grossly insufficient for a technical line involving a long turn and power line clearance, and this insufficiency directly enabled the decision to attempt the line.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'was not well rested, had only a few hours of sleep.' Sleep deprivation degrades motor coordination and reaction time, directly contributing to the inability to locate and extract the pilot chute across four attempts.

L2_preconditions.label: Winter clothing restricted BOC access
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'had winter clothing on him, which made reaching PC more difficult.' The bulky winter jacket physically impeded the jumper's ability to reach the BOC pocket, directly degrading execution of the pull sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known BOC access problem
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'already had problems with reaching his PC in that jacket before once, but got lucky that time.' The jumper was aware of a prior near-miss involving the same jacket restricting BOC access and failed to take corrective action such as switching to hand-held deployment or changing clothing.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 20-50 BASE jumps and attempted a slider-up cliff exit classified as requiring wingsuit or experienced tracker skills. Accepted progression norms require substantial cliff experience and proven tracking ability before attempting objects with mandatory high-performance tracking. This insufficient experience level directly contributed to the decision to jump an object beyond the jumper's capability.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'was reported to not have enough experience jumping slider down.' This insufficient experience with slider-down cliff jumping directly contributed to the inadequate exit technique that failed to generate sufficient forward separation from the object.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience on wingsuit type
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had approximately 12 jumps on the Vampire 4 wingsuit after transitioning from an S-Fly, which had a completely different flying style. He had not practiced sharp turning maneuvers on the V4. This insufficient experience on the specific suit directly contributed to his inability to execute the emergency turn needed to avoid the ledge, a maneuver he could perform 'on a dime' in his previous suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from prior suit mastery
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper as 'one of the fiercest wingsuit pilots on the planet' in his S-Fly suit, and witnesses believe that because his flight was going well he 'went back into hardcore mode' and chose to fly a tight proximity line. His established identity as an elite proximity pilot in a different suit appears to have driven him to fly at a level inconsistent with his limited experience on the V4, overestimating his capability in the new platform.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses reported the jumper was thinking about whether to fly proximity to the ledge the entire evening before and during the hike, indicating unresolved ambivalence about the task. His companions assessed the premature deployment was driven by panic or stress. The narrative also references the motivation of capturing a nice video as a factor in the decision-making chain.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was flying proximity for the first time, at an unfamiliar cliff he had never seen from the landing area, in a wingsuit. The combination of novel proximity flying, unfamiliar terrain, and the need to manage flight path, terrain clearance, and deployment timing simultaneously likely exceeded his cognitive capacity, contributing to the panic-driven premature deployment near the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 35 wingsuit BASE jumps on a Phantom 2, the jumper attempted proximity flying past a ledge at an unfamiliar cliff for the first time. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before terrain flying, including intimate knowledge of the specific line. His limited wingsuit BASE experience was insufficient for the demands of proximity flight near terrain, contributing to the panic-driven premature deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Site Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first jump at Kandersteg. He had not seen the landing area or the ledge from below. The domain reference emphasizes that terrain flying requires intimate knowledge of the entire line, reconnaissance from multiple perspectives, and gradual progression at a specific site. His lack of site familiarity directly contributed to deploying at an unsafe point near the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Ill-fitting wingsuit not corrected before jump
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had told people that her new Vampire 4 wingsuit did not fit well. An ill-fitting wingsuit can restrict movement and impair the ability to reach the BOC pocket, directly contributing to the failed PC deployment. Jumping with equipment known to fit poorly constitutes inadequate equipment configuration for the planned jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience with new wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: Sean had recently changed wingsuits and fellow jumpers had noticed in the weeks prior that he was not maximizing the new suit's performance. This indicates he had not yet developed proficiency with the specific suit, which directly contributed to his loss of speed/glide during the flight. The domain reference states a minimum of 40-60 skydives with a specific suit before BASE use, and that upgrading wingsuits before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had approximately 105 BASE jumps over 1-2 seasons and 70 wingsuit BASE jumps. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps with a long record of perfect exits before first wingsuit BASE, and the narrative notes the exit (High Ultimate) is recommended for experienced tracking only. The jumper's relatively rapid progression through wingsuit BASE at a demanding exit point indicates insufficient experience for the task attempted, directly contributing to the inability to manage the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Underhung exit geometry
L2_preconditions.description: High Ultimate is described as underhung, meaning the terrain below the exit extends outward, reducing the margin for recovery from a poor exit. The ramp was introduced that same day. This terrain geometry directly reduced the time and space available to recover from the head-down position, converting a recoverable exit error into a fatal impact with a ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Carl had approximately 70 BASE jumps and only 10 wingsuit BASE jumps. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE, and 100 wingsuit skydives (including 40-60 in the specific suit). With only 10 wingsuit BASE jumps, Carl lacked the experience to recognize and respond to degrading flight performance by immediately deploying. The narrative explicitly highlights his low jump numbers and insufficient wingsuit BASE experience as a contributing factor.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention
L2_preconditions.description: Despite having plenty of altitude to deploy when his performance degraded, Carl continued flying and did not pull until approximately 100m above trees. This pattern is consistent with channelized attention on the flight path or terrain ahead (including the power lines he passed over) rather than monitoring altitude and recognizing the need to abandon the flight. His fixation on continuing the flight despite clearly inadequate performance delayed the deployment decision until it was too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Carl was flying a wingsuit over a long talus slope on his first jump from this cliff. The gradually sloping terrain beneath him during degraded flight performance likely impaired his ability to accurately perceive his altitude and closure rate with the ground, contributing to his failure to deploy when he still had adequate altitude. Unfamiliarity with the visual references at this site compounded the difficulty of altitude assessment during a high-sink-rate flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only ~10 wingsuit BASE jumps and ~40 wingsuit skydives. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps with perfect exits before first wingsuit BASE. With 40 WS skydives (well below the 100 minimum) and only 10 WS BASE jumps, the jumper lacked the experience to reliably execute wingsuit exits from cliffs, directly contributing to the exit performance failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Exit Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: On the first jump of the day, the jumper exhibited bent legs during the exit — a recognized wingsuit exit error that degrades start performance. This was discussed between jumps and the jumper was reminded ('Think of your legs!') before the fatal second jump, yet the same or a related exit performance failure occurred. The jumper proceeded without adequately correcting a deficiency identified minutes earlier.

L2_preconditions.label: Snow-covered exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports 120 cm of snow at the top. A deep snow layer on the exit surface can degrade footing and launch stability, directly impairing the jumper's ability to execute a strong, stable wingsuit exit push — contributing to the failed start.

L2_preconditions.label: Terrain outcropping near exit
L2_preconditions.description: A small outcropping with bush/vegetation existed immediately below the exit point, requiring a decent push to clear. This terrain feature provided essentially zero margin for an imperfect exit, meaning the skill-based execution error directly resulted in cliff strike within approximately 1 second.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the wind's importance was 'trivialized by our confidence in our big suits.' This overconfidence in equipment capability directly drove the decision to proceed with the proximity line despite strong winds that objectively degraded wingsuit performance below terrain-clearance requirements.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: Fernando had hiked twice the day before and commented on 'feeling that second hike' during the 3.5-hour approach on jump day, which involved the entire altitude of the jump done on foot. This cumulative physical fatigue may have degraded his capacity to recognize the need to deploy or execute a deployment during the flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate flight planning
L2_preconditions.description: The three-way formation and flight path were decided at the exit moments before jumping without time for mental preparation. The line had never been flown before. This failure to adequately plan and brief the flight — especially a novel proximity line in strong winds — directly enabled the decision to commit to an unvetted line without proper assessment of terrain clearance requirements.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit experience on type
L2_preconditions.description: Fernando had only 10 total jumps (BASE and skydive combined) on the X Three wingsuit. With so few jumps on this suit, he lacked the experience to understand its performance envelope in adverse conditions, directly contributing to committing to a proximity line that required performance he could not reliably extract from the suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong headwind degraded wingsuit performance
L2_preconditions.description: The strong headwind reduced airspeed and available lift during flight, as directly experienced by the co-jumper who noted 'strong head wind and subsequent lack of speed.' This environmental degradation of wingsuit performance compressed the time and altitude available for recognizing the need to deploy and executing a pull, contributing to the failure to attempt deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Belly camera as snag hazard
L2_preconditions.description: Luke was wearing a belly-mounted camera that created a snag point during deployment. The canopy or bridle snagged on the camera during extraction while he was on his back, converting the under-rotated aerial into a total malfunction. A camera mounted on the belly is a known entanglement hazard during non-standard body orientations at deployment, and its presence enabled the fatal outcome from the skill-based error.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The bridle was visibly routed through the lateral flap of the harness, and after the PCA tightened the bridle, the bottom pin opened with canopy partially visible inside the container. A thorough pre-jump gear check — tracing the bridle from anchor to container as accepted PCA practice requires — would have detected this incorrect routing before exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit and proximity experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had 'no experience in proxy flight and fairly new to wingsuiting,' and describes the jumper as 'rushing to base jump and getting into the wingsuit far too early.' This indicates the jumper's training and experience were clearly insufficient for the proximity flying task attempted, directly contributing to the decision to fly a line beyond their capability.

L2_preconditions.label: Anabatic/thermal airflow degradation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly notes this was the fourth jump of the day in the afternoon, and states that all fatalities at this site occur in the afternoon when airflow has changed and cold air moves into the valley.
