Anabatic flow and shifting valley wind conditions in the afternoon would have reduced effective glide performance and altered the aerodynamic environment along the flight path, directly degrading the jumper's ability to maintain speed and glide through the turn and subsequent terrain clearance.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had a 'wrong appreciation of the height' over a grassy dome, indicating a visual illusion or misperception of terrain proximity. Featureless grassy terrain is a recognized cause of visual illusions that impair altitude/distance judgment, directly contributing to the altitude/terrain misjudgment identified at L1.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Proximity Flying Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Dan was 'only a novice wingsuit Proxy flyer' despite being a good wingsuit BASE jumper. Proximity flying near terrain requires substantially greater experience than standard wingsuit BASE, and his novice-level proximity skills were insufficient for the line attempted, directly contributing to his decision to fly a line he could not clear.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The deceased flew into the wrong crack, approximately 20 meters from the correct line. He misidentified the terrain feature he was flying into, believing it was the intended line when it was actually a dead-end crack. This geographic misidentification directly enabled his commitment to the fatal proximity line.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Proxy flying demands intense focus on the terrain feature being flown past, which channels attention toward maintaining the flight line at the expense of monitoring altitude and terrain clearance. The narrative states the jumper 'lost height awareness,' consistent with attention fixated on the proximity task to the exclusion of altitude monitoring.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the exit as one of the top 3 most advanced wingsuit jumps in the world, one that has killed even advanced pilots with hundreds of WS BASE jumps. The jumper's exit is characterized as 'unskilled,' directly indicating that the jumper's experience and skill level were insufficient for this specific task. This insufficiency in capability contributed to the decision to attempt an exit that was clearly beyond the jumper's level.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS Exit Skill
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly characterizes the exit as 'too steep (unskilled),' indicating the jumper lacked the trained proficiency required for a proper wingsuit exit at this demanding site. The jumper was unable to transition from exit to flight, a skill that requires extensive wingsuit BASE experience and practiced technique at progressively more demanding exits.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 22 total BASE jumps (10 slider-down prior to the trip plus 12 in the valley), with no reported slider-up non-wingsuit cliff experience. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE. This profound experience deficit directly enabled both the decision to attempt wingsuit BASE and the selection of an unsuitable object.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's lack of wingsuit BASE experience and training directly produced the head-high slow flight pattern. Without the progression through sub-terminal tracking, slider-up cliff jumps, and extensive wingsuit skydiving specific to BASE preparation, the jumper lacked the skill to manage angle of attack and airspeed, resulting in near-stall flight — a recognized fatal error pattern that proper wingsuit BASE progression is designed to prevent.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper, a very experienced skydiver (AFF instructor, tandem master, thousands of skydives), ignored advice from fellow travelers and experienced jumpers in the valley, did not register for a landing card or obtain local site information from the SBA, and proceeded to attempt wingsuit BASE on approximately his 23rd BASE jump. This pattern of disregarding multiple sources of guidance and accepted progression norms indicates the jumper unreasonably overestimated their capability based on skydiving experience, which does not substitute for proper BASE training and progression.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to obtain site information
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper did not register for a landing card with the SBA and had no information from the SBA about exit profiles. Obtaining local site information is a standard preparatory action at Lauterbrunnen, and this failure directly contributed to the selection of Yellow Ocean as a wingsuit exit without understanding that its altitude profile was insufficient for wingsuit flight, particularly at the jumper's experience level.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Other jumpers in the valley had observed that most of his exits in his Prodigy were too head-high and that he was not flying as quickly as he should be on exit. This recurring exit technique problem — falling straight down rather than achieving adequate airspeed — was a known deficiency that was never corrected before he attempted a more demanding suit and exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Video review and witness analysis concluded the jumper likely became fixated on an object (a tree near his impact point) or lost altitude awareness during the tracking phase. This attention management failure directly contributed to his failure to initiate deployment at a survivable altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper performed a roll onto his back and then back to belly during the multi-way exit sequence. This maneuver, combined with tracking at a steeper angle below his partner, likely disrupted his spatial orientation and altitude perception, contributing to his failure to recognize the need to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was Amber's first wingsuit BASE jump. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and at least 100 regular BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump. Her poor flight performance — failing to build forward speed and not outflying the talus — and her loss of altitude awareness are consistent with the fundamental inexperience of a first wingsuit BASE attempt. A first wingsuit BASE jump represents insufficient experience for the demands of wingsuit terrain flying, altitude management, and deployment timing in a wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Altitude Awareness Failure
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'she must have lost altitude awareness,' and the possible factors list includes 'altitude awareness.' The jumper failed to monitor her altitude during wingsuit flight, reaching back to pull only when much too low. This loss of situational awareness regarding altitude directly enabled the late deployment. Task saturation from managing a first wingsuit BASE flight likely contributed to channelized attention away from altitude monitoring.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper planned the double gainer as a tribute to his deceased friend Daniel Moore, who had done his first double from the same exit point. This emotional motivation to complete the aerial contributed to his proceeding despite visible fear and hesitation (shaking, going back and forth, screaming expletives), overriding the rational signals to abort.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Aerial
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's attention was focused on completing the double gainer rotation rather than monitoring altitude. When the second rotation stalled, his 'Oh shit!' exclamation upon returning to belly-to-earth indicates he only recognized the altitude deficit at that moment, consistent with channelized attention on the aerial task at the expense of altitude awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The tumbling motion of the double gainer, particularly when the second rotation stalled, likely caused vestibular disorientation that impaired the jumper's ability to perceive altitude and orientation. His verbal reaction upon regaining belly-to-earth position indicates he did not recognize his altitude situation until visual reference was restored, by which point it was too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 30 BASE jumps and 1 season of experience while attempting a slider-up cliff jump requiring sub-terminal tracking, off-heading correction skills, and line twist management. Per accepted progression norms, slider-up cliffs are among the most complex BASE objects, requiring well-developed tracking and object avoidance skills typically built through extensive practice at simpler objects. With 30 total BASE jumps, the jumper's experience level was clearly insufficient for the demands of this task, directly contributing to the inadequate separation and inability to correct the 180-degree off-heading with line twists.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was performed at night at a very dark site with inadequate ground lighting and minimal moonlight. The narrative states that aerials can be disorienting even on a sunny day, and that in the low light conditions he may have lost orientation or altitude awareness during the rotation. This visual impoverishment during an aerial maneuver would have degraded his ability to judge when to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Stash bag configuration hazard
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper placed a stash bag on his back beneath the rig with an elastic adjusting strap that had approximately 6 inches of free slack when empty. The elastic line was found pulled taut, indicating it likely interfered with pilot chute extraction. This equipment configuration created a snag hazard that contributed to the deployment failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions at night
L2_preconditions.description: The site was very dark, ground lighting had not been recently replaced, and the moon was low or already set. These conditions directly impaired the jumper's ability to maintain spatial orientation and altitude awareness during the aerial maneuver, contributing to the late or failed deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes the jumper's behavior as 'almost inexplicable' — he had plenty of time and altitude to deploy safely but continued flying as altitude progressively deteriorated. The companion speculates about possible 'brain lock' and wanting to 'enjoy the ride as long as he could.' This pattern is consistent with channelized attention on continuing the flight/completing the line to the exclusion of monitoring altitude margin and making the deployment decision. The sustained failure to act over an extended period despite obvious cues suggests fixation rather than a simple misjudgment.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'it was a while since his last jump' despite 10 BASE seasons of experience. The companion speculates he may not have been 'jumping regularly enough to realize he was in a bad situation right after the exit.' Lack of currency in wingsuit BASE flying could have degraded his ability to accurately assess his altitude margin and glide performance in real time, contributing to the delayed deployment decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was rushing to jump because it was getting dark ('don't be in a rush to jump because it is getting dark'). The time pressure of fading daylight at 9pm drove the decision to jump in winds that exceeded site limits rather than waiting for better conditions or walking down.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong winds degraded tracking and deployment
L2_preconditions.description: Winds beyond site safety limits directly degraded the jumper's ability to maintain a stable track and achieve proper body position at deployment. Excessive wind at a cliff object mechanically impairs tracking performance and body stability, contributing to the poor unstable track and bad body position at pull time described in the narrative.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience on new suit at aggressive angles
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that while Shaun was hyper-current and experienced in wingsuit BASE, he did not have as much experience flying his particular new suit at the highly aggressive angles required for the Brevent lines. The new suit had different flight characteristics from his previous suit, specifically in how a pilot would fly very steep lines. This insufficient familiarity with the suit's behavior at extreme angles directly contributed to the technique errors in managing the dive-to-flight transition — the over-exposure of the wing's top surface and the abrupt AoA correction that produced the fatal stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet/muddy exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the exit point was still draining water and conditions were very wet and muddy after many rainy days. This degraded the exit surface traction, directly causing the jumper to slip during the wingsuit launch.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper misjudged the start arc, which was longer than he thought, indicating he misperceived his position and trajectory relative to the terrain during the critical transition to the left line. This misperception of spatial position directly contributed to the failed terrain flight execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Donald's decision to jump was influenced by watching the first jumper successfully clear the exit, which gave him confidence despite not feeling fully comfortable. The narrative states he 'probably decided to jump because the first guy did and made it' and that he 'got the confidence to jump by watching the first guy get off.' This borrowed confidence from observing another jumper's performance served as a misplaced motivational driver, contributing to his proceeding with an exit he did not execute with sufficient force.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: Donald was listening to music at the exit point, which prevented him from hearing repeated attempts by his companion on the observation deck to communicate with him. This external distraction eliminated the opportunity for critical information exchange about exit technique requirements before he jumped, directly contributing to his inadequate exit execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Channing used old and degraded break-cord that had been torn apart rather than cut, compromising its integrity before use. He rigged an improvised static line system with two short pieces of break-cord tied together and attached far toward the end of the bridle, increasing dynamic loading. These specific preparation failures created the conditions that caused both the primary knot to slip and the secondary cord to break prematurely.

L2_preconditions.label: No pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Bryan did not receive a gear check from any other jumper prior to exit. A gear check is an accepted practice that could have detected the rigging condition (e.g., closing loop large enough to admit the pin eye, or improper pin/loop interaction) that caused the pin lock malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: Bryan was slightly rushed to finish his pack job because the other jumpers had finished packing and were returning to the bridge, and he wanted to ride in their van back to the top. This time pressure contributed to the conditions under which the rigging error occurred and the gear check was omitted.

L2_preconditions.label: Cloud cover obscured visual references
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level clouds were present at exit altitude, and the hole in the clouds closed immediately after exit. Loss of visual ground references due to cloud immersion would have directly impaired the jumper's ability to assess altitude and judge correct deployment timing, contributing to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator describes the notch line as 'the new standard of being an alpha male in the valley' and compares the jumpers to 'moths to a flame,' indicating that social status and competitive motivation within the local wingsuit community drove the decision to fly this line despite its minimal margin and lack of meaningful flight benefit.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator describes the notch line as 'the new standard of being an alpha male in the valley' and compares the jumpers to 'moths to a flame.' This indicates a social/competitive motivation — maintaining status among elite wingsuit pilots in Yosemite — that drove both jumpers to commit to a line that an experienced peer had explicitly told them was not worth the risk.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had not flown the X2 wingsuit for one year, despite being current on a different suit (Aura). This lack of currency on the specific suit directly contributed to his degraded flight performance — flying slowly and going off-route — which is the consequence of the decision to fly non-current equipment.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had not flown the X2 wingsuit for one year. Different wingsuits have different BOC access characteristics and arm-wing configurations affecting the reach and deployment motion. His lack of currency on this specific suit directly contributed to his failure to execute the PC deployment on the first two attempts.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper went slightly off-route, then realized he wouldn't be able to continue on his line. By the time he recognized the need to deploy, he was in a position requiring immediate action, and the delayed recognition of his off-route position contributed to the late deployment attempts.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was only the jumper's second wingsuit BASE flight (the first being from the same exit). Wingsuit BASE requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps as prerequisites, and proficient wingsuit terrain flying demands extensive progressive experience. His second-ever wingsuit BASE jump placed him at a critically early stage of progression where the skills needed for effective wingsuit flight execution and managing terrain proximity had not been developed.

L2_preconditions.label: Jumping wet equipment
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that prior to this jump, Ian had landed in the river and gotten his gear wet. The domain reference explicitly states 'Never jump wet equipment.' The wet PC and canopy likely delayed PC inflation, contributing to the over-rotation and bridle entanglement during the aerobatic deployment. The extra weight of wet equipment coupled with a relatively small 36" PC for a ~4-second delay further degraded the deployment sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost / Unfamiliar Line
L2_preconditions.description: Magnus had never flown the Barn Line before and planned to follow another jumper who had. When the other jumper exited and Magnus did not follow, he lost the benefit of a guide on an unfamiliar line. Flying an unknown proximity line solo, without the visual reference of a lead jumper, directly degraded his ability to manage speed, glide, and terrain clearance along the route.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was a 'Very Technical exit with a few second rock drop not for a beginner or intermediate wingsuit basejumper,' directly indicating the exit required advanced wingsuit BASE skill. The jumper's failure to execute an adequate push from this technical exit is consistent with insufficient experience for the specific demands of this object. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before attempting highly technical exits with minimal terrain clearance margins.

L2_preconditions.label: Extreme wind at exit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper jumped in 'extremely high winds' that directly caused the unstable exit (flipped after exit). The strong wind at the cliff exit physically disrupted the jumper's body position and prevented recovery, directly degrading execution of the exit and flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit familiarity
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only skydived the wingsuit twice before attempting a wingsuit BASE jump. Accepted practice requires 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE use. This gross insufficiency of suit-specific experience directly degraded the jumper's ability to execute a successful start arc and transition to flight from a technical exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Exit surface condition
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'possibly slipped on exit,' indicating the exit surface condition (wet, icy, or loose rock) degraded his footing and directly caused the unstable exit that he was unable to recover from.

L2_preconditions.label: Katabatic airflow degraded flight
L2_preconditions.description: After 3:30pm in late December, line 7 was in shadow with radiational cooling producing katabatic (downslope sinking) airflow. This atmospheric condition actively degraded the wingsuit's available lift and effective glide performance during the critical approach to the entrance point, making the already challenging flight path significantly more difficult and directly contributing to the stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction from phone call
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe Jean was on a phone call via a Bluetooth communicator during the wingsuit flight. The narrative explicitly identifies talking while flying as challenging and potentially distracting. This external stimulus diverted attention during a flight phase requiring precise airspeed and position management, contributing to the execution failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded landmark recognition
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe Jean had only flown line 7 a few times and had not familiarized himself with how waypoints and landmarks appeared under snow cover. He had explicitly stated after a previous flight that he was unsure of his position before making the entrance. Snow-altered visual references impaired his ability to accurately judge speed, position, and the appropriate moment to commit or abort, contributing to the stall during the critical approach.

L2_preconditions.label: Motivation to impress companion
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe Jean held the other jumper in high regard and had expressed excitement about showing him line 7. He exited immediately after the other jumper, passed him, and went straight for the challenging line rather than flying conservatively. This desire to demonstrate the line to an admired companion is a clearly identifiable misplaced motivation that contributed to committing to the aggressive flight path rather than exercising caution.

L2_preconditions.label: No abort plan established
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that Philippe Jean and his mentor/companion did not discuss how or when to abort if things did not go as planned on line 7. The absence of a pre-briefed abort plan for a challenging proximity line meant there was no predetermined decision point at which to abandon the approach, contributing to his commitment to the entrance point despite insufficient energy.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE season and was attempting a jump at an object described as 'very much a blue to black diamond' — a highly technical site requiring advanced site assessment skills. This insufficient experience level directly contributed to the inadequate evaluation of the landing area, wind conditions, and ocean hazard that constituted the L1 decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level fog and clouds degraded the jumper's ability to see terrain and maintain spatial orientation during wingsuit flight. The police report explicitly states low-level fog could have increased the chance of impact from low visibility. These conditions directly impaired the visual references essential for wingsuit terrain flying, contributing to the terminal freefall impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Tracking Performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'had felt improvements on his tracking' and may have 'pushed it too far,' and that 'the road is a landmark on that jump and it's common for people to want to track past it.' This suggests channelized attention on tracking performance and the goal of passing the road landmark, to the exclusion of monitoring altitude — directly contributing to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions from goggles
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'had complained about his ski-goggles he jumped with not being optimal to see through.' Impaired visual acuity from suboptimal goggles could have degraded his ability to accurately assess altitude and terrain proximity during the tracking phase, directly contributing to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Gusty crosswind at exit
L2_preconditions.description: Winds were gusty and visible on the grass at the exit point. These gusty crosswind conditions directly degraded the jumper's exit stability, contributing to the slip and immediate loss of control during the two-move dynamic exit in a large wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate field repack procedure
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper re-closed the container at the exit point without removing the bridle and PC from their stow — a critical step in the repacking sequence. This failure to follow the complete procedure when re-securing the dislodged pin created the bridle misrouting that caused the pin lock malfunction. A systematic gear check after the field adjustment would have detected the improper bridle routing before exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: Gary's wingsuit flight on both days exhibited stalled/near-stalled flight from exit, indicating he lacked the fundamental skill to achieve efficient wingsuit flight. The witness noted that basic wingsuit BASE concepts (exit technique, angle of attack, flying in non-thermic conditions) were 'brand new to him,' and his request to join a technical multi-way formation further demonstrated a gap between his actual capability and the demands of the task. His inability to produce adequate forward speed on two consecutive attempts indicates insufficient wingsuit proficiency for the object.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Gary repeatedly described the Chief as 'basically like Brento' despite the witness knowing this was incorrect, dismissed corrective information from another source, and planned to immediately travel to France for Brevant after returning. After a near-miss requiring an emergency pitch and highway landing, he packed slider-up with his wingsuit again and even asked to join a technical multi-way formation. This pattern of dismissing risk information and overestimating his own capability directly contributed to his decision to repeat a jump that had clearly exceeded his ability the previous day.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of currency from injury break
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Gary had taken a break from jumping to recover from an injury prior to this trip. While the exact duration is not specified, the witness noted it as a relevant factor. Returning from an injury-related break degrades currency and physical readiness, which would have contributed to his poor wingsuit execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site evaluation
L2_preconditions.description: Gary characterized the Chief as similar to Brento and rejected corrective information about the actual jump profile, indicating he did not adequately evaluate the site's demands before committing to the jump. Proper site evaluation—understanding the flight profile, power line obstacles, and non-thermic morning conditions—is a required preparatory action that would have informed a more appropriate decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Incomplete exit angle briefing
L2_preconditions.description: Although exit angles for camera flying were discussed on the cable car, when the plan changed at the top (the lead jumper would fly his normal line without camera-flyer procedures, and JVH chose the right side), there was no further discussion of exit angles at the exit point. This incomplete re-briefing after the plan change left the specific exit angle for JVH's position unresolved, directly enabling his push onto the same axis as the first jumper.

L2_preconditions.label: Burble from lead jumper
L2_preconditions.description: After entering the first jumper's flight path due to the L1 exit angle error, JVH encountered the lead jumper's wake turbulence (burble) approximately 4 seconds into the jump. This caused visible shaking, clear loss of power, and a leftward swerve, mechanically degrading his wingsuit's aerodynamic performance and placing him in the energy-depleted state from which he attempted to fly out rather than deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience for terrain flying
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy had approximately 100 wingsuit skydives (almost all on a different suit, the P3) and only 2 skydives on the V3 before taking it to BASE. His previous Brento jumps were described as 'not very good, rather unsteady.' This level of experience and demonstrated proficiency on the V3 was clearly insufficient for choosing a committing terrain line requiring turns over ridges, per accepted wingsuit BASE progression norms.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from recent success
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that two steady straight-out jumps the previous day 'gave him confidence' to attempt the more committing turning line over ridges. This confidence was disproportionate to his overall demonstrated ability on the V3, which was characterized by unsteady performance across most of his Brento jumps.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient suit familiarity for terrain management
L2_preconditions.description: Jimmy had only 2 skydives and approximately 13 BASE jumps on the V3, a recently purchased suit that was not his primary wingsuit. His lack of familiarity with the V3's flight characteristics — speed range, stall behavior, and recovery — directly contributed to his inability to manage speed and glide near terrain, as he progressively slowed the suit into lateral instability rather than maintaining speed or converting to a dive.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had worked a night shift as an air traffic controller, slept only a few hours, then completed nine tandem jumps and one fun jump before inspecting and rigging her BASE gear. The narrative concludes that 'all information points toward extreme fatigue played a large role in this rigging mistake.' This level of sleep deprivation and physical exertion directly degraded the cognitive capacity needed to execute the critical rigging check.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper opened both rigs to inspect them before jumping. Despite this inspection in a lit area, the disconnected bridle-canopy attachment was not detected. The gear check failed to catch the existing rigging deficiency — a bridle not attached to the canopy — which is a fundamental verification step in BASE equipment preparation.

L2_preconditions.label: Reduced thermal lift
L2_preconditions.description: The late afternoon timing meant diminished anabatic/thermal lift compared to the jumper's earlier flights that day. The narrative explicitly states there was less lift than on previous jumps, when he had cleared the same ridge point by 10-15 meters. This reduced environmental lift directly degraded the jumper's effective glide performance, contributing to his inability to maintain sufficient altitude to clear the ridge.

L2_preconditions.label: Snow on exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states there was snow on the exit point, and the jumper's family believes he slipped on it. Snow on the exit surface directly degraded footing and caused the slip that produced the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong winds degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: The report states strong winds were present at the time of the jump. At a high-altitude exit point (Titlis), strong winds can directly destabilize a wingsuit exit by disrupting the jumper's body position during launch, contributing to the legs going over the back and the resulting unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'misjudged his position' relative to the ridge. This indicates he misperceived his spatial relationship to the terrain during flight, which directly led to the selection of a line he could not clear.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to make an emergency pull despite being on a collision course with terrain. The narrative's statement that he 'failed to make an emergency pull' after misjudging his position suggests he was fixated on the tracking line or terrain avoidance to the exclusion of recognizing the need to deploy, or recognized it too late to act.

L2_preconditions.label: Slick exit surface conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'the mud was still a bit slick' at the exit area. Slick footing from mud directly degraded the jumper's ability to execute a clean, powerful exit, contributing to the 'too head low and not far away' exit that resulted in clipping the ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper struggled on the approach, turning a 3-hour hike into a 5-hour trip. He stated he would 'rather jump than hike down' due to being tired. This physical overexertion from the prolonged approach likely degraded his physical capacity to execute a demanding wingsuit exit requiring precise body positioning and a strong forward push.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's stated motivation for jumping was to avoid hiking down ('I'd rather jump than hike down') rather than being driven by readiness and confidence in conditions. This misplaced motivation to avoid the descent contributed to proceeding with the jump despite physical fatigue, directly setting up the conditions for the poorly executed exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Tailwind-induced downdraft at exit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes a tailwind over the mountain creating a slight downdraft at the exit. For a wingsuit exit requiring forward separation to clear a ledge 150 meters below, a downdraft would actively degrade the jumper's ability to achieve forward flight and increase the altitude consumed during the start arc, directly contributing to the insufficient separation from the already-compromised exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Glide
L2_preconditions.description: The eyewitness states the jumper 'lost his speed slowly and didn't realise he was losing speed.' He was flying at maximum glide approximately 100m above terrain, indicating his attention was directed toward maintaining glide ratio and terrain clearance rather than monitoring airspeed — a critical parameter for wingsuit flight. This channelized attention on glide performance to the exclusion of speed awareness directly enabled the progressive speed loss that led to the stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With 65 wingsuit BASE jumps and only 3 BASE seasons, the jumper was flying an Aura 2 — an intermediate-to-advanced wingsuit — that the eyewitness explicitly describes him as a 'beginner with this suit.' The eyewitness directly attributes the fatal outcome to insufficient experience: 'He had not enough experience with this suit to realise he was losing speed.' Recognizing progressive speed decay and managing the speed-glide tradeoff near terrain are skills that develop with extensive wingsuit experience, and this jumper lacked the experience to detect the deteriorating energy state before it became unrecoverable.

L2_preconditions.label: Wind-induced exit instability
L2_preconditions.description: Winds of 5-10 m/s at the exit point directly degraded the jumper's ability to achieve stable wingsuit flight after exit. The witness observed the jumper tumbling immediately from exit until impact, indicating the wind mechanically disrupted body position and prevented flight initiation or recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was only the jumper's second jump ever with the C-Race wingsuit and second jump from Brento. The C-Race is a high-performance wingsuit. Accepted progression norms require 40-60 skydives with a specific suit before BASE use, and extensive practice pulls in all body positions. With only one prior jump in this suit, the jumper had minimal familiarity with the BOC reach mechanics and recovery procedures specific to this wingsuit, directly contributing to the failed PC extraction and the inability to return to stable flight for a reattempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: A 7 mph crosswind from the jumper's right to left was present at the exit point. This crosswind actively contributed to the unstable, head-low exit by introducing an asymmetric aerodynamic force during the launch, degrading the jumper's ability to achieve a stable exit attitude and contributing to the subsequent corkscrew.

L2_preconditions.label: Descending air degraded flight
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes that when the jumper turned toward the landing zone, he 'may have caught some cooler descending air and he lost his flight characteristics very quick.' This atmospheric disturbance — katabatic or sinking air on the cooler side of the ridge — actively degraded his wingsuit's aerodynamic performance at the critical moment, directly contributing to the loss of speed and glide that constituted the L1 skill-based failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Possible visual impairment from selfie stick
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was wearing a selfie stick camera mount on his helmet, which the narrative notes may have disturbed his vision and depth perception. If this impaired his ability to accurately judge terrain proximity and clearance margins, it would have degraded the situational awareness underlying his decision to commit to the challenging left-sector line over the ridge.

L2_preconditions.label: Limited experience on wingsuit model
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 10-12 jumps on the Vampire Alpine wingsuit. Limited familiarity with a specific suit's performance envelope — its start arc, glide characteristics, and speed range — degrades the jumper's ability to accurately assess whether a given proximity line is within achievable margins, directly contributing to the decision to fly a line that exceeded the suit's performance or the jumper's ability to extract that performance.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 25 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The BOC extraction skill—cleanly reaching past container features to extract the PC without routing it through obstructions—is a fundamental motor skill that develops through repetition. With only 25 jumps, the jumper had limited opportunity to develop the reliable muscle memory needed for consistent, error-free BOC deployment, particularly while wearing a tracking suit that may alter reach dynamics.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet/damp exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'perhaps he slipped (there was previous rainy days),' indicating the exit surface may have been wet or damp from recent rain. This surface condition would have directly degraded the jumper's footing during the exit, contributing to the unstable/head-down departure.

L2_preconditions.label: Burble from lead jumper
L2_preconditions.description: Jumper 2's wingsuit burble directly disrupted Ben's flight, causing instability and a dramatic decrease in groundspeed. The narrative explicitly states Ben flew through the burble, became unstable, and lost groundspeed from which he could not recover. This environmental disturbance actively degraded his ability to maintain adequate airspeed at a critical moment when he was already flying at high GR with limited energy reserves.

L2_preconditions.label: Downwind degraded exit recovery
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states there was downwind coming from the right, and that the jumper 'couldn't manage to recover quickly because of the downwind.' The wind actively degraded his ability to recover from the head-low exit attitude by pushing him toward terrain and reducing effective forward separation, directly contributing to the failed recovery from the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Cold-induced loss of hand dexterity
L2_preconditions.description: Kristian was without gloves for at least 12 minutes before the jump and wore only thin gloves for the final 5 minutes. He was observed trying to blow his hands warm before exit. The narrative states it is 'truly believed that the cold made him lose the feelings in his fingers and hands,' directly impairing his ability to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 3 BASE seasons and was flying a Vampire Sukhoi, a high-performance wingsuit. The narrative describes flight 'a bit on the slow side,' consistent with insufficient experience to fly the suit efficiently. Reliable BOC location during full-flight wingsuit deployment is a skill requiring extensive wingsuit-specific training (hundreds of practice pulls in all body positions per accepted norms). The repeated failed attempts to locate the PC handle suggest the jumper had not developed the muscle memory and technique required for consistent BOC extraction in this suit, indicating insufficient training/experience for the specific wingsuit BASE task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify gear stowage before exit
L2_preconditions.description: The video shows Eddie handling and checking the bridle routing prior to exit, but he failed to verify that the right-side carabiner remained securely stowed after climbing over the railing and sitting on the top rail. A pre-exit gear check that included verifying the slings and carabiners were secure would have detected the partially dislodged carabiner before it could interact with the bridle.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only one BASE season, approximately 50 slider-down jumps, and only 2-3 slider-up jumps from this antenna (his first slider-up object). Accepted progression norms require extensive experience with aerials from forgiving objects before attempting them from technical objects, and slider-up jumping requires significant progression beyond slider-down. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task of performing a gainer from a slider-up antenna.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'performed poor gainers, some of them were rather unstable and uncontrolled,' indicating a sustained pattern of inadequate aerial execution that he was aware of through repeated personal experience. Despite this known recurring deficiency, he continued to attempt gainers and escalated to performing one from a more demanding object (slider-up antenna) without correcting the underlying skill deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Health issues
L2_preconditions.description: The report lists 'Health issues' as a possible factor. At age 50 with documented health issues, a physical or physiological condition may have degraded the jumper's ability to execute the PC extraction, contributing to the inability to locate the pilot chute in the BOC pocket.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 150 skydives, which is below the generally accepted minimum of 200-250 before beginning BASE. With 143 BASE jumps on an insufficient skydiving foundation, core skills including reliable BOC extraction under stress may not have been adequately developed, contributing to the failed pull attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The video evidence and mentor's analysis indicate Oscar was fixated on repeating his morning proximity line and buzzing the trees for at least the last 10 seconds of flight. His mentor states he was 'so focussed on flying the line that he didn't pay attention to how this flight was going differently.' This channelized attention on the intended line prevented him from recognizing his degraded speed/energy state and executing the available escape to the right until it was too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The witness estimated that the jumper's visuals were fixed on the landing area and she did not see the ledge coming. This channelized attention on the landing zone to the exclusion of terrain proximity awareness directly contributed to the late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: This was the jumper's first terminal tracking jump from a slider-up cliff, with only 70 BASE jumps and 2 BASE seasons. Terminal slider-up cliff jumping with a tracking suit requires sub-terminal tracking proficiency and altitude awareness skills developed through progressive cliff experience. The jumper did not effectively establish her track or maintain adequate terrain awareness, consistent with insufficient BASE experience for this specific task.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 19 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season, and was attempting slider-up terminal cliff jumps requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking in a tracking suit he had only recently acquired. Accepted progression norms indicate that sub-terminal tracking from terminal walls requires substantially more experience, and the narrative explicitly states 'there is nothing that can simulate subterminal tracking other than subterminal tracking' and recommends balloon jumps prior to terminal walls. His experience level was clearly insufficient for the task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Turning Tendency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'did have a tendency to turn once he was up to speed,' indicating a recurring sub-terminal tracking deficiency observed across previous jumps. This known performance issue was not corrected before continuing to jump terminal cliff objects where tracking separation is mandatory for survival.

L2_preconditions.label: Adverse physiological state
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Rob's 'judgement may have been somewhat impaired by a bad stomach and altitude.' A gastrointestinal illness combined with altitude effects could have degraded his physical execution capacity, contributing to the weak push and poor body position on exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was 'a case of jumping from the wrong exit point.' The jumper exited from a position he apparently believed provided adequate clearance, but the ledge gradually protruded further on the right side where he stood. This indicates he misjudged or was unaware of the terrain geometry at his specific exit position relative to the ledge below — he was effectively at a different position relative to the terrain than he believed.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper appeared to focus on turning himself 180 degrees away from the cliff rather than monitoring altitude. This channelized attention on the heading correction task came at the expense of altitude awareness, directly contributing to the fatally late deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 40 skydives, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ before beginning BASE. This extreme deficit meant the jumper lacked fundamental freefall body position, canopy piloting, and deployment skills necessary for any BASE jump, let alone a wingsuit BASE jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had no First Jump Course, no mentorship, and no BASE training of any kind. The narrative explicitly states he had no training whatsoever and was soliciting strangers to take him to exit points. This complete absence of training meant he lacked knowledge of BASE-specific equipment requirements, deployment techniques, object avoidance, and emergency procedures.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper attempted a wingsuit BASE jump with zero prior BASE jumps. Accepted progression requires at least 100 BASE jumps with a long record of perfect exits before attempting wingsuit BASE. The jumper had none of the prerequisite BASE experience for any advanced discipline.

L2_preconditions.label: Skydiving gear used for BASE
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper used a skydiving container (Micron) with a skydiving main, reserve, and deployment bag rather than BASE-specific equipment. This configuration lacks the rapid deployment characteristics required for BASE jumping and was identified as the cause of death. Proper BASE preparation requires BASE-specific gear configured for the object type.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate jump planning for aerials
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the aerials were not mentioned to the jump partner prior to the jump and the jumper had not performed aerials from this specific exit point before (only his second jump from Nose 3 that day). Performing aerials from an unfamiliar exit without coordinating with the jump partner represents inadequate pre-jump planning and briefing, creating conditions that enabled the over-rotation error.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Lluis had 80 wingsuit BASE jumps and was jumping a wingsuit he had 'not many jumps on,' from a 'fairly tricky exit.' Accepted progression norms require extensive familiarity with a specific suit (30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential) before jumping challenging exits. The combination of limited experience on the specific suit and a tricky exit point contributed to the unstable, head-low, asymmetric exit execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet/muddy exit surface conditions
L2_preconditions.description: It had rained the night before and the exit area was muddy and wet, though the main exit rock was reported dry. For a running/stepping exit, surrounding wet and muddy conditions at a 'fairly tricky exit' could have degraded footing stability during the few-steps exit, contributing to the head-low and asymmetric body position.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper consistently overestimated his ability to manage tight proximity lines, flying at what the narrative describes as '110%' despite repeated feedback from an experienced flying partner that he needed more margin. He declined a 2-way with an experienced terrain flyer because that flyer wouldn't be 'charging hard enough,' and chose to fly an aggressive line through unfamiliar terrain on what was most likely his first flight there. His own acknowledgment that 'ego' was his main weakness, combined with his persistent pattern of flying beyond acceptable margins despite awareness and external feedback, demonstrates an enduring overestimation of capability relative to the tasks attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct low-margin flying
L2_preconditions.description: Despite weekly reminders from an experienced flying partner over three months, awareness that his ego drove him to fly with insufficient margin, and his own stated intention to fly with more safety margin, the jumper failed to consistently correct his pattern of flying at or beyond the edge of acceptable risk. The narrative describes a recognized, recurring pattern ('110%') that he acknowledged but did not adequately address, constituting normalization of deviance over the season.

L2_preconditions.label: No training or experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was a 15-year-old with no instruction on any technique — no skydiving experience, no first jump course, no mentorship. The complete absence of any training or education meant the jumper had no basis to understand that skydiving gear is unsuitable for BASE or how to configure equipment for a fixed-object jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct low-pull habit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had a documented pattern of opening lower than he should and was explicitly warned about this by his mentor and other jumpers. Despite these warnings, he failed to correct the behavior, continuing to deploy at dangerously low altitudes.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 75 BASE jumps across one season and only 5 wingsuit BASE jumps, the jumper was attempting slider-up tracking suit jumps from terminal cliffs in Lauterbrunnen. His rapid progression through 47 jumps on his first trip and continuation to terminal cliff objects with a tracking suit within one season suggests experience insufficient for the demands of the task, contributing to poor judgment on deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate PC handle configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The PC handle was found in an open configuration, which is noted as a known factor where fingers can get stuck. This equipment configuration created conditions enabling the failed PC extraction at the critical moment of deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Unzipping
L2_preconditions.description: Marcus was focused on unzipping his wingsuit while flying away from the landing area after deployment. The unzipping task diverted his attention from monitoring altitude and position relative to the only viable landing zone, contributing to his failure to recognize he was too low to return to the road until it was too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE season, 90 BASE jumps, and only 5 skydives with a tracking suit. This site required mandatory tracking at a slider-down cliff with ~4 seconds of usable delay and known strong coastal winds requiring specific timing. The combination of limited BASE experience and near-zero tracking suit experience made the jumper poorly equipped to assess the risk of jumping this site in strong wind conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong winds degraded execution
L2_preconditions.description: Strong coastal winds during the warmer part of the day (when winds are known to be stronger at this site) likely degraded the jumper's exit stability, tracking performance, and/or object separation during freefall. The wind actively impaired execution capacity regardless of the decision to jump, contributing to the fatal impact with the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate PC packing configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The pilot chute was fully stuffed into the BOC with only the carbon fibre tubular handle sticking out, placing the bulkiest part of the PC inside the elastic. The operating organization explicitly stated they do not recommend this packing method. This configuration directly contributed to Jon's inability to locate and/or extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to detect misrouting during gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumping partner performed a rig check before exit but failed to detect the bridle misrouting through the lateral band. A thorough gear check tracing the bridle path from anchor to container would have caught this rigging error before the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 100 skydives and 50 BASE jumps over 2 BASE seasons. The accepted minimum before beginning BASE is 200+ skydives, and the jumper was rigging a static line deployment—a specialized configuration requiring thorough knowledge of bridle routing. This insufficient experience base directly contributed to the execution error of misrouting the bridle through the lateral band.

L2_preconditions.label: Wet exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the exit had wet grass, which the reporter believes caused Eric's feet to slip, sending him into a front flip/scorpion position. The wet surface directly degraded his ability to execute a stable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Experience with Long Flights
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that over recent seasons the jumper had mainly jumped only 'short' jumps (20-25s Lauterbrunnen style) where pilots build speed, release it, and pull when speed is exhausted. This jump required sustained speed management over a longer (~30s) flight with a committed crux section. His lack of experience with longer committed flights in a tracking suit directly contributed to his failure to recognize the abort point, as the speed-management pattern from short jumps did not transfer to this profile.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 10 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The delayed brake release after a riser correction is a fundamental canopy handling sequence that requires practiced muscle memory. With only 10 jumps, the jumper lacked the repetition needed to execute this sequence instinctively under pressure, directly contributing to the slow transition from risers to brakes.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 10 BASE jumps, the jumper lacked the experience to accurately assess whether he could reach the main landing zone from a degraded altitude. Experienced jumpers develop the ability to judge glide performance relative to obstacles through repeated practice; at 10 jumps this judgment was undeveloped, contributing to the decision to continue forward rather than use an emergency landing area.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate static line configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to properly configure the static line system for this specific site. The static cord loop was too long for the bridge, and the excess bridle between the two break cord loops was not stowed with a rubber band, creating a system that was excessively long with free-moving bridle and cord. This inadequate pre-jump rigging setup created the conditions that enabled the bridle to entangle with his foot during exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Possible fatigue from prior activity
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports that before this jump, the jumper had climbed a 260-metre antenna during the night, performed a slider-up jump, then packed slider-down and drove to the bridge. The analyst who reviewed the GoPro footage noted the jumper appeared 'more stressed than usual' in the video. This sequence of overnight climbing, jumping, repacking, and driving may have contributed to degraded attention during rigging setup and exit execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Possible movement restriction from gear
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative notes the jumper was carrying crampons that may have filled his suit pockets and tightened the wingsuit, potentially reducing his movement capabilities and degrading his exit push. A companion recalled seeing his suit pockets pretty filled on previous jumps. While stated as a possible factor rather than confirmed, this is a plausible biomechanical limitation that could have contributed to the weaker-than-usual exit push.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper lost his helmet during the pitch sequence, which diverted his attention from the critical task of deploying the PC. The narrative explicitly states he reached for his helmet with one hand while still holding the PC by the handle, and the analyst's assessment is that 'he probably kept the PC in the hand because he was thinking of his helmet.' This distraction directly caused the delay in releasing the PC that allowed the bridle/PC entanglement to develop.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate helmet securing
L2_preconditions.description: The helmet chin-lock was found open, indicating the helmet was not properly attached before the jump. The helmet coming off during the pitch sequence created the distraction that caused the jumper to hold the PC rather than deploy it cleanly, directly enabling the bridle-under-arm entanglement.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost / Misjudged Position
L2_preconditions.description: Flying at high speed in a wingsuit at low altitude with low sun straight into the wall, Gonsholt may have misjudged his position relative to the pre-established reference point. However, the narrative states from GPS and POV data that he was clearly below the reference point, and the decision to continue is explicitly noted. The evidence is insufficient to distinguish between misperception of position and a conscious choice to press on despite recognizing the deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Exit-Opening Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The report explicitly states that Gonsholt's 'experience in exit opening was low,' meaning he lacked experience in evaluating and flying first-time or newly opened sites. This directly contributed to his inability to correctly assess when to abort. The two other jumpers had over 40 combined years of BASE experience and a long history of opening new exit points, highlighting the gap. Despite approximately 200 wingsuit BASE jumps and impressive dynamic flying skills, the specific competency of evaluating marginal new lines and making abort decisions at unfamiliar sites was underdeveloped.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes Jeremiah as visibly nervous at the exit point (giving a 'nervous chakra' instead of a fist bump), yet he proceeded with the technical line rather than choosing the safer alternatives suggested by his companion. His companion noted that 'sometimes we trap ourselves into a plan' and that Jeremiah likely would have changed plans if directly asked. This pattern — fixating on the notch line despite acknowledged uncertainty and available safer options — indicates a self-imposed commitment to the plan that overrode his own rational assessment.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Jeremiah repeatedly asked about the notch line during the hike (at the halfway mark and again near the rock gully), despite being told he lacked the necessary data (laser numbers, GPS data, subterminal turn performance data) and being offered safer alternatives. His companion described this as fixation on flying through the left notch. This channelized focus on the technical line appears to have excluded adequate consideration of the safer flight options that were explicitly suggested.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: Jeremiah's last wingsuit BASE jump was approximately 10 months prior (November 2019). While he had made 200 wingsuit skydives in the interim, wingsuit skydiving does not maintain the site-specific judgment, subterminal performance awareness, and terrain-proximity decision-making skills required for wingsuit BASE. This extended gap in wingsuit BASE currency may have degraded his ability to accurately assess whether his suit and skills could manage the technical notch line.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient mountain WS experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had roughly 100 WS BASE jumps total but was described as 'not experienced in the mountains' and was from Paris. He had only approximately 20 jumps on his Corvid wingsuit. This insufficient mountain-specific experience directly contributed to his selection of a demanding line requiring >3:1 glide with a long NPZ under conditions (high altitude, morning thermals) that required experienced mountain judgment to evaluate.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient suit experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 20 jumps on his Corvid wingsuit. Insufficient familiarity with the suit's flight characteristics and speed management contributed to his inability to fly at adequate speed and glide, as witnessed by observers who were immediately concerned by his slow flight.

L2_preconditions.label: High altitude and thermal conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jump occurred at high altitude with thermals producing and that morning jumps are tricky because of high altitude and thermals. Reduced air density at high altitude degrades wingsuit performance (lower available lift and glide for a given airspeed), and thermal activity creates variable atmospheric conditions. These environmental factors directly degraded the jumper's ability to maintain adequate speed and glide over the plateau.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative establishes a sustained pattern of aggressive proximity flying at the top end of what is possible, with multiple peers noting this style was 'not sustainable.' Despite this feedback, the jumper continued flying aggressively close to terrain, indicating he unreasonably overestimated his ability to manage the margins involved. This overconfidence in his capacity to fly near-max-glide proximity lines contributed to the decision to attempt clearing the pinnacle with inadequate margin.

L2_preconditions.label: Waterfall-induced canopy collapse
L2_preconditions.description: After the canopy edge contacted the waterfall due to insufficient separation, a surge of water struck the left end cells causing collapse, and katabatic/downdraft airflow associated with the waterfall entrained the stalled canopy into the falls. The waterfall's water flow and associated air currents actively degraded canopy performance beyond what the off-heading alone would have caused, converting a correctable off-heading into a total canopy loss.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Video evidence revealed the jumper was agitated at the exit point, repeatedly resetting foot placements and touching his pilot chute multiple times, indicating excessive deliberation about peripheral concerns. This over-focus on foot placement and pre-exit rituals may have diverted attention from the core exit execution (push direction and body position), contributing to the over-rotation on launch.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper initially showed no interest in Cima Capi on two prior opportunities, remaining adamant about jumping Brento main exit. By Monday evening he requested to join, and after a poor exit experience at Pilastro (weak push, head low), he took only one recovery day before requesting to join the Cima Capi group again. This escalating desire to join the more experienced group at a new exit, despite his recent poor exit performance at a similar object, suggests social motivation contributed to proceeding with the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With 130 BASE jumps and 4 seasons, the jumper was a debutant to the Cima Capi exit—a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking with a tracking suit. His only prior experience at a comparable exit (Pilastro) two days earlier resulted in a self-reported weak push and head-low instability. His limited experience with technical cliff exits contributed to the over-rotation and inability to recover a stable body position.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate exit point assessment
L2_preconditions.description: Fabrizio chose an exit he had never jumped before, finding it via GPS point. The terrain under the exit did not provide sufficient altitude to recover from a head-low exit. Jumping a new exit without prior reconnaissance or knowledge of the terrain profile below — specifically whether it afforded adequate recovery margin for a wingsuit start arc — constitutes inadequate jump preparation that directly enabled the fatal consequence of the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only ~25 BASE jumps limited to antenna and slider-down cliff experience, Yaroslav attempted his first slider-up cliff jump. Accepted progression norms require extensive object avoidance practice at forgiving objects (spans), sub-terminal tracking competence developed over many jumps, and gradual progression before attempting slider-up cliffs. His experience level was clearly insufficient for this task.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: Yaroslav's mentor was explicitly described as an inexperienced jumper. He did not complete a recognized first jump course or follow accepted training pathways (Croatian S + Brento plan was abandoned due to COVID). Experienced jumpers warned him about the dangers of his jumps without proper training, and the narrative establishes his training was inadequate for the progression he attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Yaroslav was explicitly warned by experienced jumpers about the dangers of his progression without proper training. He acknowledged his approach was 'roulette' but continued, stating he hoped his paragliding skills and psychology would compensate. This represents a sustained pattern of proceeding despite awareness of a known deficiency in training and progression, not a single-instance failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Filming
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'always cared about the quality of his videos' and suggests the camera angle and crowd angle may have been more important to him than the wind angle. This focus on filming likely diverted attention from proper deployment technique and wind management, contributing to the gentle/inadequate PC toss.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was wearing a 'long camera mount' that provided a snag point for the bridle during the entanglement. Jumping with a protruding camera mount on a 60-meter slider-down jump in strong crosswind conditions created the physical mechanism by which the weak PC toss became a fatal entanglement rather than merely a hesitation.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind at exit
L2_preconditions.description: Strong crosswind blowing from the jumper's right to left actively carried the gently thrown PC across his body, directly enabling the bridle wrap. While the jumper's decision to jump in these conditions is a separate matter, the crosswind mechanically contributed to the entanglement by redirecting the inadequately thrown PC into the bridle/body/camera mount.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper had been drinking at a bar earlier that evening before deciding to jump. Alcohol impairment degrades judgment and decision-making capacity, directly contributing to the decisions to attempt a BASE jump with skydiving gear and without adequate training or experience.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 147 skydives, below the accepted 200+ minimum prerequisite for beginning BASE jumping. This insufficient foundation contributed to his inability to recognize that skydiving equipment is fundamentally unsuitable for BASE applications.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 prior BASE jump and no evidence of completing a first jump course or having competent mentorship. The narrative explicitly states he lacked the knowledge to understand the critical differences between skydiving and BASE gear and the hazards of the specific situation. Without BASE-specific training, he could not recognize that a skydiving canopy in slider-up configuration from a 14-story building was non-survivable.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 BASE jumps, 60 skydives, and 1 BASE season. With 60 skydives he was far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, and with only 5 BASE jumps he had minimal experience with static line deployment procedures. This insufficient experience base directly contributed to the execution error in bridle routing or pilot chute handling during the static line deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform final gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that under the first hypothesis, the jumper did not perform a final check of the static line routing before jumping, which would have caught the bridle passing in front of his right arm. Accepted practice for static line jumps explicitly requires that every jumper have a knowledgeable person verify they are clear of rigging before jumping by tracing the bridle by hand from shrivel flap to anchor. This omitted check would have detected and prevented the routing error.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'in the rush before the jump' as a contributing factor to the jumper not performing a final check of the static line routing. This haste directly contributed to the execution error by causing the jumper to skip the verification step that would have caught the bridle misrouting.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 50 skydives and 40 BASE jumps in 1 BASE season. With skydiving experience far below the accepted minimum of 200+ jumps before beginning BASE, and limited BASE experience, the jumper lacked the foundational knowledge and judgment to correctly evaluate the lee-side wind shadow as dangerous. This insufficient experience directly contributed to the flawed decision to jump in strong wind conditions that all experienced local jumpers recognized as unsuitable.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumpers had 'only a few days to be in that area,' indicating time pressure from a limited trip window contributed to the decision to jump despite strong winds. This external pressure drove them to relocate to a secondary exit and proceed rather than wait for suitable conditions, even after experienced local jumpers had all declined to jump that day.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 10 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. Instant off-heading correction via rear risers or brakes is a skill that requires extensive practice at forgiving objects (spans) to develop the muscle memory needed for reflexive response. With 10 jumps, the jumper had insufficient experience to have developed reliable off-heading correction skills, directly contributing to the failure to arrest the 180° turn before object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps and was known to have recurring problems with wingsuit starts. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE, and extensive practice before attempting sites that do not tolerate poor exits. With 3 WS BASE jumps and acknowledged start deficiencies, his experience was clearly insufficient for the site attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps and known problems with starts, the jumper lacked the skill and experience to recover from a poor wingsuit exit and transition to effective flight or timely deployment. The execution failure in the exit directly reflects insufficient wingsuit BASE experience.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Start Problems
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of his problems with wingsuit starts and had been warned by others, yet he did not correct this deficiency before attempting a site that required competent exits. This sustained failure to address a known recurring performance issue directly enabled the poor exit that produced the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Bryce had done 70-80 wingsuit BASE jumps in one month, was 'very confident and feeling great in his suit,' and 'got quickly bored in Lauterbrunnen and started to fly proximity.' Despite warnings from close friends about his 'bad habit of pulling low' and a prior consequence (water landing at Altissimo), he continued the practice. This pattern of escalating confidence overriding external feedback and personal experience directly enabled his decision to delay deployment initiation.
