L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: On the morning of the accident, Bryce told his partner Gabe that he 'felt tired.' The investigation summary explicitly identifies that long BASE trips can be exhausting and lists physical/mental tiredness as a contributing factor. This fatigue may have degraded his judgment regarding deployment timing, contributing to his decision to initiate the pull sequence too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct low-pull habit
L2_preconditions.description: Bryce had developed a recognized pattern of pulling low. He had already experienced a consequence (water landing after a low pull from Altissimo weeks earlier), and two close friends explicitly warned him about this habit the day before the fatal jump. Despite this awareness and feedback, he failed to correct the behavior, directly enabling the repeated decision to delay deployment initiation.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 16 BASE jumps, 2 BASE seasons, and 144 skydives (below the accepted minimum of 200+ before beginning BASE). A slider-up cliff requiring a 10-second delay with mandatory tracking represents an advanced BASE task. The complete absence of freefall control and tracking — fundamental skills for slider-up cliff jumping — indicates experience and skill clearly insufficient for this task, directly contributing to the failure to track and maintain body control.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 144 skydives, the jumper had not reached the generally accepted minimum of 200-250 skydives before beginning BASE jumping. Skydiving develops the core freefall body control and canopy piloting skills that are prerequisites for BASE. The inability to control body position or initiate tracking during a 10-second freefall reflects this foundational skill deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Turbulent thermal conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Another BASE jumper present that day reported conditions were extremely turbulent and noisy to fly in with a lot of sink and lift closer to the ground. Strong anabatic conditions were present at exit. These atmospheric disturbances (turbulence, sink, and lift) would have directly degraded the jumper's ability to build speed, maintain a stable flight path, and achieve adequate terrain separation during the critical start phase in a one-piece tracking suit, which has minimal margin compared to a wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 BASE season and 100 BASE jumps, and the narrative indicates 'No or little training' as a possible factor. Arranging a PCA from an unqualified non-jumper and disregarding advice on PC sizing reflect a knowledge gap about PCA requirements and equipment selection that accepted BASE training and mentorship would have addressed.

L2_preconditions.label: Unqualified PCA assistant not briefed/replaced
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to ensure a qualified person performed the PCA. Accepted practice requires a knowledgeable person to hold the bridle (not just the PC), maintain position, and release at the correct moment. Using a non-BASE-jumper without apparent training or briefing as the PCA assistant was a preparatory failure that directly enabled the early release and resulting malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient terrain flying experience
L2_preconditions.description: The witness explicitly states 'I have not known Jimmy to be a terrain flyer,' yet the selected exit point required immediate terrain flying from the start arc with zero margin for error. This indicates the jumper lacked the specific wingsuit terrain flying experience and skill set required for this object, which directly contributed to the decision to attempt a jump that demanded capabilities beyond his experience level.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Despite being told explicitly by the witness that the start arc transitions directly to terrain flight, that even the experienced witness could not outfly the terrain beyond the minimum needed, and despite not being a terrain flyer himself, the jumper proceeded with the jump. The narrative indicates he believed he was 'current and ready' for his first wingsuit BASE jump in Moab, suggesting he unreasonably overestimated his ability to manage a zero-margin terrain flight that exceeded his demonstrated skill set.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: After clipping the ledge and being sent into violent unstable rotation, the narrative states Thomas was 'probably disoriented or in shock' during the 6 seconds before wall impact. The violent physical impact with the ledge and resulting tumble likely triggered a startle/freeze response or spatial disorientation that prevented him from initiating deployment despite available time.

L2_preconditions.label: Absence of anabatic lift
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred at 08:50, before significant solar heating could generate anabatic updraft on the cliff face. A fellow jumper who jumped at 07:50 the same morning reported being closer to the wall than usual. The absence of the lift normally present during later conditions reduced the vertical and horizontal separation margin, directly contributing to Thomas clipping the ledge by only 20cm despite an otherwise corrected track.

L2_preconditions.label: Novelty-seeking pattern
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper 'had a passion for beautiful flying lines and was always chasing novelty: he rarely jumped the same exit or flew the same line several times' and that 'his lines were committed, which he was totally aware of.' This enduring behavioral pattern of seeking novel, committed lines directly contributed to the decision to fly a deeper, lower, unfamiliar variation on this jump, reducing his margin and eliminating outs.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify bridle routing before exit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to perform a final gear check that would have detected the bridle routed around his leg before exiting. The witness describes that he himself nearly made the identical error and caught it only by chance (a bird landing drew his eyes to his crotch area), indicating that a deliberate trace of the bridle from shrivel flap to anchor — which accepted static-line practice requires — was not performed or was inadequate. A proper pre-exit bridle check would have caught and corrected the misrouting.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 100 skydives (well below the accepted minimum of 200+) and approximately 50 BASE jumps across 2 seasons. This was described as only his second slider-down stowed deployment from a cliff. The domain reference establishes that slider-down cliff jumping requires refined exit and deployment skills developed through extensive progression. His limited experience directly relates to the insufficient exit push and immediate pull that failed to generate adequate object separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's mentor explicitly identified complacency as a contributing factor, attributing it to the jumper's positive personality traits and confidence from paragliding experience that led to reduced vigilance about execution quality. This complacency contributed to the insufficient exit push and immediate deployment without adequate separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper had 'a winter without jumping,' indicating an extended period without BASE activity before this fatal jump. This lack of currency would have degraded the motor skills and timing needed for a quality exit push and proper deployment sequence from a slider-down cliff, directly contributing to the execution errors identified in L1.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration for site
L2_preconditions.description: The mentor's report states the cliff was 'not really a cliff for slider down' — at 500m and slightly overhanging with extensive cliff below, the site characteristics combined with a slider-down configuration (which produces rapid openings with less time for heading correction) created conditions where the insufficient exit push became fatal. A slider-up configuration with delay and tracking would have provided greater object separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit proficiency
L2_preconditions.description: A close friend stated the jumper 'wasn't very good at flying' the tracking suit and experienced 'significant rolling and pitching movements,' indicating he had not developed the skill level required for proximity flight. He had been warned on multiple occasions that he was pushing beyond his skill level, confirming his capability was insufficient for the task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence despite warnings
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was 'pushing a lot in the last period before the accident' despite being told on more than one occasion that he had not yet developed the right skills. This pattern of disregarding repeated warnings from peers indicates an overestimation of his own capability that drove the decision to fly a proximity line beyond his ability.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known instability problem
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of recurring instability issues (significant rolling and pitching) in his tracking suit and had been warned multiple times about pushing beyond his skill level, yet he continued to attempt proximity lines without correcting the underlying performance deficiency.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced a very head-low exit from the same object the day prior and was aware of the problem, yet returned the next day without correcting the underlying technique issue. His comment about having enough separation to do an emergency front flip and still clear the ledge indicates he recognized the head-low tendency but rationalized it rather than addressing it.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal turbulence degraded flight
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly describes hitting 'a wall of lift' when crossing the ridge onto the sun-exposed south side, flying in 'turbulent air and probably a bit slower than expected,' and the witness observing Chris 'struggling a bit.' These thermal conditions mechanically degraded flight performance—reducing speed and available lift—which directly contributed to the jumper being unable to execute his intended line or maintain adequate margin when he committed to the right turn into terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Personality Style
L2_preconditions.description: Friends explicitly described Jonny as having 'a tendency to go all out and fly right at the limit,' indicating an enduring behavioral pattern of pushing margins. This stable pattern directly contributed to the decision to fly closer to terrain than on previous occasions on a technical line with multiple sharp turns.

L2_preconditions.label: Katabatic airflow over glacier
L2_preconditions.description: The companion reported that katabatic wind over the glacier produced sinking air that pushed them toward the ground, particularly noticeable at the end of the glacier where the impact occurred. This atmospheric condition mechanically degraded the jumper's available lift and effective glide, directly contributing to the inability to maintain terrain clearance despite the chosen flight path.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple close friends and the companion explicitly stated that the jumper habitually flew close to terrain despite his low experience level (80 BASE jumps, 3 seasons) and was told on numerous occasions to slow down. This pattern of repeatedly disregarding warnings from experienced peers indicates the jumper systematically overestimated his ability to manage proximity flying relative to his actual skill and experience level.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to heed repeated warnings
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was warned on numerous occasions by multiple close friends and his companion to stop flying close to terrain given his experience level. Despite this sustained feedback over time, he failed to correct the behavior, representing a pattern of inaction in the face of known deficiency that directly enabled the fatal proximity flying decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 80 BASE jumps across 3 seasons and no reported wingsuit BASE jump count, the jumper was flying proximity terrain lines that his peers explicitly identified as beyond his experience level. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before proximity terrain flying, and the narrative confirms multiple experienced jumpers assessed his experience as insufficient for the flying he was attempting.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct recurring head-low exits
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'He use to have head low exits,' indicating a known, recurring performance deficiency. The jumper's failure to correct this pattern over time directly contributed to the fatal exit execution error.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient aerial training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's partner explicitly noted he 'only ever jumps his wingsuit' at cliff objects, and she told him performing a gainer was 'a stupid idea' for that reason. The domain reference states aerials require extensive training, careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object. Performing a gainer from a slider-up cliff without established competence in slick aerial exits represents insufficient training for the specific task attempted, directly contributing to the over-rotation and instability.

L2_preconditions.label: Camera mount as snag hazard
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's helmet was equipped with an Insta360 camera on a unicorn extension mount with a protruding 6cm bolt. After the gainer-induced 180-degree off-heading opening, the front left riser swoop loop snagged on this bolt, converting a survivable object strike sequence into a sustained, unrecoverable entanglement against the wall. While the camera mount did not cause the gainer decision, the failure to identify and mitigate this specific snag hazard in pre-jump preparation directly amplified the consequences of the L1 error.

L2_preconditions.label: Shaded couloir obscured terrain details
L2_preconditions.description: The exit point was located in a shaded couloir at that time of day, which the narrative explicitly states 'made it really difficult to see the details of the landscape.' This degraded visual condition directly impaired the jumper's ability to assess the terrain from the new exit point, preventing detection of the ledge on the flight trajectory and contributing to the inadequate assessment underlying the decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial disorientation from visual illusion
L2_preconditions.description: The sub-terminal flight occurred in a shaded couloir where hidden terrain details only became visible once in flight. The jumper's inability to perceive the immediate aerodynamic effect of his control inputs at sub-terminal speed—reinforcing an input that would become excessive as airspeed built—reflects a perceptual mismatch between control input magnitude and changing flight dynamics in visually degraded conditions where terrain references were unreliable.

L2_preconditions.label: Difficult exit surface condition
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the exit is 'very uncomfortable and difficult to stand there if you climb over the rail.' This degraded exit surface condition directly impaired the jumper's ability to execute a clean exit, contributing to the foot snag on the rail and resulting head-low over-rotation.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction from hand camera
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the hand-mounted camera 'may have distracted him or cost him some time in terms of reaction and correcting the off heading.' While stated as a possibility, a hand-mounted camera is a plausible source of channelized attention or distraction that could have delayed the heading correction after the 130-degree off-heading opening.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 25 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. While he had 800 skydives, 25 BASE jumps represents very early progression. The exit required managing an uncomfortable stance and clearing a rail — a technically demanding exit that a jumper with limited BASE experience would be less equipped to execute cleanly. The poor exit technique (sideways stance, foot catch) and failure to correct a 130-degree off-heading on a static line cliff jump are consistent with insufficient BASE experience for this object.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 50 skydives and 1 BASE season with 100 BASE jumps. The domain reference states the accepted minimum is 200-250 skydives before beginning BASE, and that aerials 'exponentially increase danger' requiring 'extensive training,' 'careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object.' With only 50 skydives, the jumper lacked the foundational freefall body control and spatial awareness needed for aerobatic BASE exits, directly contributing to the over-rotation and inability to manage the deployment sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to verify bridle routing after reclose
L2_preconditions.description: After the pin popped and the container was re-closed, accepted practice requires checking the bridle routing all the way from PC to canopy attachment points. The jumper did not perform this verification, which would have detected the mis-routed bridle before the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'we can only assume she didn't know about this malfunction,' indicating the jumper likely lacked knowledge of how re-closing a popped pin can create a bridle misrouting pin lock. This specific knowledge gap about the pin lock failure mode from improper bridle routing during reclose directly contributed to the rigging error.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The canopy opened 90° off-heading to the right while the jumper's body had been turning left, and the added inertia of heavy ski boots and skis caused a violent body rotation back to the right. This sudden, forceful physical disruption likely overwhelmed the jumper's ability to execute the correct toggle inputs, resulting in releasing only the left toggle instead of performing a proper heading correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient ski-BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative indicates ski-BASE was a dream the jumper was fulfilling, suggesting limited or no prior ski-BASE experience. The added mass of ski boots and skis fundamentally altered opening dynamics, producing a violent body rotation that a jumper experienced in standard BASE but not in ski-BASE would not have anticipated or trained to manage. This inexperience with the discipline-specific forces directly contributed to the failed heading correction.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states this was the jumper's first jump after 3 years of not jumping. This extended period without jumping likely degraded his situational awareness, physical coordination, and judgment regarding terrain hazards while scouting the unstable exit area, contributing to the fall.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred at night (23:45). Darkness would have directly impaired the jumper's ability to visually locate the BOC pilot chute handle, contributing to the failure to extract and deploy the pilot chute.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Flight Recovery
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper appears to have fixated on recovering his wingsuit flight path during the oscillating corrections, channeling attention on regaining forward flight to the exclusion of monitoring altitude and terrain proximity. The narrative states there was sufficient time for an emergency pull but he likely 'felt he could out fly the terrain,' indicating his attention was locked on the flight problem rather than shifting to deployment as a survival action.

L2_preconditions.label: Habitual low terrain separation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that the jumper 'often separated late from the terrain' and that 'the altitude that he gained from the flare was often needed to get a good pull altitude.' This is a sustained pattern of flying with minimal margin, relying on flare performance for adequate deployment altitude, representing a normalization of deviance that directly enabled the fatal decision to continue flying low with insufficient energy on this jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Altitude awareness degraded without GPS
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative notes the jumper did not have his Flysight (GPS) on this flight. The Flysight provides audible altitude and speed feedback during wingsuit flight. Its absence removed a tool that could have alerted him to his low altitude and reduced speed state, contributing to his failure to recognize the insufficient margin before initiating the flare at approximately 80m or lower.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses and video analysis indicate the jumper fixated on the far flight line after initiating the aggressive right turn, failing to monitor the close terrain (pillar) during the maneuver. He showed no corrective action before impact, consistent with attention channelized on distant reference points to the exclusion of proximate obstacle awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Atmospheric conditions degraded flight performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes headwind gusts, high temperature producing thermals, and shade on the wall creating differential heating — conditions that actively degraded the wingsuit's aerodynamic performance. The witness reports the jumper was 'sinking a bit after the 4th second,' experienced 'not much power from the 4th to 9th second,' and 'a bit turbulent flight due to the stronger wind at mid-flight.' These atmospheric conditions (anabatic flow, headwind, turbulence) mechanically reduced the jumper's effective glide ratio and available lift below what was needed to clear terrain, directly contributing to the insufficient margin that characterized the L1 decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper tracked past and below his partner without apparent awareness that deployment was overdue, even as his partner had already opened. The multi-way tracking format and the slight convergence toward his partner's side may have channelized his attention on the track itself or on maintaining separation, to the exclusion of monitoring altitude. The approximately one-second hesitation with his hand on the pilot chute further suggests he was not monitoring altitude throughout the track and recognized the situation late.

L2_preconditions.label: Reduced lift and headwind vs prior day
L2_preconditions.description: On the accident day there was no wind at the exit and hot thermals were producing, compared to the prior day's 5-10 mph quartering headwind and significant lift on the face. The first jumper reported being noticeably lower than the day before under the same conditions. The absence of the headwind component and anabatic lift that had been present the previous day mechanically reduced the jumper's effective glide performance and available lift over the ridge, directly contributing to the fatal terrain impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit problem
L2_preconditions.description: Thor had experienced a series of bad exits in his Alpine 3 wingsuit in the weeks prior and was aware of the problem, explicitly stating he wanted to stop jumping the suit and was waiting for a new one. Despite this known recurring deficiency, he continued jumping the same suit from a technical exit rather than correcting the problem first.

L2_preconditions.label: Misplaced motivation to fix exits
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes Thor declaring 'I have to fix my exits!' before proceeding to jump solo from a technical exit point, despite acknowledging problems with the suit and having a series of bad launches. This suggests a pressure or motivation to resolve the issue by jumping through it rather than stepping back to a more forgiving environment, which directly contributed to the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way planning
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was briefed as a two-way with the second jumper performing a gainer above Jorge, who jumped first with a short delay. This plan placed an aerial-performing jumper directly above a jumper who would be under canopy shortly after exit, with insufficient vertical/horizontal separation to protect against the foreseeable consequence of a delayed deployment during the aerial. The briefing and jump order created the conditions that made the bridle entanglement fatal to Jorge.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Nathan 'seemed to be in a rush' while gearing up, which is consistent with haste contributing to the bridle misrouting. Rushing through rigging procedures reduces the deliberate attention needed to correctly route the bridle, directly enabling the skill-based execution error.

L2_preconditions.label: No gear check by qualified person
L2_preconditions.description: Nathan's companion was not a BASE jumper and could not have noticed the misrouted bridle. For a TARD/static line deployment, accepted practice requires a knowledgeable person to verify the jumper is clear of rigging before jumping. The absence of a qualified gear check meant the misrouting went undetected.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct habitual rigging practice
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Nathan had a 'habit of gearing up with the bridle outside of the BOC,' which created recurring exposure to misrouting risk. This habitual practice over multiple jumps was a known deficiency that was never corrected and directly enabled the fatal misrouting on this occasion.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Rear camera footage shows very little input was made to avoid the cliff. The narrator states 'I can only think she possibly froze or just didn't react quick enough.' This suggests a startle response or freeze reaction upon experiencing the off-heading opening facing the wall, which prevented her from executing the heading correction skills she had recently been practicing.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 100 BASE jumps and only one prior jump from this specific cliff (5 years earlier), the jumper was attempting a slider-up terminal cliff jump. Her previous jump from Brento involved only a sub-5-second delay with a slow slider, and she herself recognized she needed more airspeed for better separation. The combination of limited cliff experience and the demands of a terminal slider-up cliff with mandatory tracking contributed to her inability to execute an effective off-heading correction under pressure.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The video analyst states 'it honestly looked like he got object fixated, once his body was heading towards the rock, and he just froze. There was not any attempt at pitching at all.' This describes a freeze response — a temporary inability to act in the face of rapidly approaching terrain — that directly prevented Kenn from executing any deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator states Kenn 'looked extremely tired from the long climb/hike to the exit' and that it was 'one of the first hot days this season' compounding the fatigue. Physical exhaustion from the approach in heat would have degraded his cognitive and physical capacity, plausibly contributing to his failure to initiate deployment or respond to the developing situation.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator explicitly references 'a long hiatus' and states that 'being uncurrent can have a huge impact on decision making.' While exact duration is not specified, the narrative treats his lack of currency as a significant contributing factor, and the narrator — his only BASE contact — had no idea of his current state. Lack of currency would degrade the practiced, automatic deployment responses needed in a wingsuit BASE environment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration
L2_preconditions.description: Kenn used a Velcro container for a slider-up wingsuit BASE jump. The domain reference explicitly states Velcro rigs are 'contraindicated for terminal, wingsuit, and aerobatic jumps due to risk of premature opening at high airspeeds' and should 'only be used for low-airspeed slider-down applications.' While the fatal outcome was failure to deploy rather than premature opening, this configuration choice reflects a fundamental preparation failure. The possible factor 'unable to locate pilot chute' suggests the equipment configuration may have contributed to his inability to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was expecting to jump that day and had hiked since 5am with that intention. When the planned site did not materialize and a marginal alternative was found, he was 'very fast to gear up' and 'suddenly appeared over my shoulder on exit ready to jump,' bypassing the planned sequence where another instructor was gearing up to go first. The narrative explicitly identifies that 'expectations did not meet reality for the day, and B. was expecting to jump,' indicating a commitment to jumping that drove his decision to proceed despite the site exceeding his demonstrated ability.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'chose to jump into a situation he hoped he would perform perfectly, without enough repetition and consistency' and that 'experience or confidence gained over 10 years ago should not equate to modern wingsuit jumps.' Despite having 1500 BASE jumps and 200 WS BASE jumps, his exits throughout the course had not been consistent. He overestimated his ability to execute at this unforgiving site, insisting he was ready when the instructors had not cleared him as one of the few students suitable for this exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE currency/proficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Despite 20 BASE seasons and 200 WS BASE jumps on paper, the jumper's exits throughout the multi-day course 'had not been great but manageable' with only 'moderate improvement' despite instructor intervention. The narrative explicitly states that 'experience or confidence gained over 10 years ago should not equate to modern wingsuit jumps,' indicating his current wingsuit exit proficiency was insufficient for the task. This degraded execution capability directly produced the unstable exit and failed recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Bypassed planned jump sequence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper geared up rapidly and appeared at the exit ready to jump while an instructor was still gearing up to go first. He insisted he was ready, bypassing the coordinated sequence that would have provided him additional information about the site's demands. This failure in coordination contributed to his decision to jump a site that exceeded his demonstrated ability.

L2_preconditions.label: Compressed progression timeline
L2_preconditions.description: With 425 BASE jumps compressed into a single season, the jumper's progression was extremely rapid. The domain reference establishes that time in the sport is as important as jump numbers and that rushed progression is a recognized risk factor. The 6-second tumble without recovery to a tracking position from a slider-up cliff indicates that the body control and recovery skills required for cliff jumping had not matured sufficiently despite high jump numbers, consistent with skills that require time and varied experience to consolidate.

L2_preconditions.label: No skydiving or BASE training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had no identified connection to skydiving or BASE jumping, no USPA membership, and no apparent training or experience of any kind. He lacked all foundational skills—skydiving proficiency, first jump course, mentorship—required to rig, pack, and deploy BASE equipment. This complete absence of training directly enabled the misrigging that caused the fatal malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct chronic speed/exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's close friend confirmed they had discussed his problems with head-low exits and slow flying speed for many months. Rather than addressing the root cause through training, he attempted to compensate by purchasing a C-Base wingsuit. This persistent failure to correct a known, recurring performance deficiency directly contributed to the poor exit and slow flight that led to the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence in leading demanding line
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to lead five other jumpers on a demanding proximity line despite chronic, well-documented problems with exit technique and flight speed. His willingness to go first and guide others on terrain where even skilled pilots in higher-performance suits found little margin indicates he unreasonably overestimated his ability to manage this specific line given his known deficiencies.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit skill for terrain
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had a documented chronic inability to achieve adequate speed and proper exits in wingsuit BASE, as confirmed by his closest jumping partner. His friend explicitly stated he needed to 'fix your problems with training and not just changing a suit,' indicating his skill level was insufficient for the demanding proximity flying he was attempting. This skill deficit directly produced the poor exit and slow flight that caused the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience for line
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 2 BASE seasons and this was his first wingsuit jump from this exit point. His prior experience at this site was limited to two jumps with a less performant Onesie suit on a different, less technical line. Flying a technical proximity line requiring precise altitude management through a gate and into a steep trench demands site-specific wingsuit experience that he did not possess, directly contributing to his selection of an unsuitable line.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper was 'probably too much focused on reaching the second landing area of the jump' and 'lacked complete awareness of the safety altitude.' This channelized attention on the distant landing goal degraded his monitoring of altitude and terrain clearance, directly contributing to his failure to deploy at a survivable altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Wingsuit BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had approximately 200 BASE jumps and 100 wingsuit skydives, with wingsuit BASE jump count not reported. The narrative explicitly states he 'didn't have yet the experience to assess the speed range of the suit, nor to realize he was flying too close to the stall point.' This specific knowledge and skill gap — inability to recognize and manage the suit's speed envelope — directly contributed to his flying too slowly near stall. Per accepted progression norms, a wingsuit BASE jumper must have full command of the suit's speed range, which requires extensive experience with the specific suit; the narrative positively establishes this experience was lacking.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 25 BASE jumps and 1 BASE season. The aggressive 270-degree low-altitude turn demonstrates a lack of canopy piloting judgment specific to BASE environments — managing a canopy surge after DBS toggle release and executing appropriate corrections in the extremely limited altitude available from a bridge jump. With only 25 BASE jumps, the jumper had insufficient experience to have developed the canopy handling skills and altitude awareness needed to recognize and arrest an escalating low-altitude turn before it became unrecoverable.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had not studied any previous videos, Google Earth, or other tools before his first jump, and was on only his second jump at this site. After making the right turn, he was 'pretty blind to what was coming next' — he did not know the terrain geometry beyond the second ridge. This lack of terrain awareness meant he could not accurately perceive his position relative to the terrain during flight, directly contributing to his committed line into unseen terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: No site reconnaissance or flight planning
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to study any videos, Google Earth imagery, or other planning tools before jumping the site. Accepted practice for wingsuit BASE requires intimate knowledge of the entire flight line from exit to landing, including reconnaissance on foot or via other means. This failure to perform basic site reconnaissance directly enabled the decision to fly a committed proximity line into terrain he had not verified.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 20 wingsuit BASE jumps, the jumper attempted a flat, highly committed proximity line requiring precise terrain knowledge and energy management at an unfamiliar site. Accepted progression norms require extensive wingsuit BASE experience before flying near terrain, with gradual progression over many sessions at known sites. Twenty wingsuit BASE jumps is clearly insufficient for committed proximity flying, and this experience deficit directly contributed to his inability to manage speed, glide, and terrain clearance.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe had approximately 90 BASE jumps over 4 years, almost all slider-down and slick, before attempting wingsuit BASE with a Freak suit. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 BASE jumps with a long record of perfect exits and comfort with sub-terminal tracking before first wingsuit BASE. His BASE experience was overwhelmingly in a fundamentally different discipline (slider-down slick) that does not develop the skills required for wingsuit deployment, including reliable BOC location at speed in a wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience for Site
L2_preconditions.description: Philippe had approximately 40 wingsuit BASE flights before attempting Magland, which the narrative describes as 'much more technical than what he was used to' — shorter, more positive terrain, with civilization directly below. This was only his second flight from this exit. The combination of very limited wingsuit BASE experience and an unfamiliar, more demanding site contributed to his inability to execute the deployment sequence under pressure.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Philippe 'most likely didn't find his pilote chute, panicked, never found it.' The initial failure to locate the BOC handle appears to have triggered a panic response that prevented him from recovering and completing the deployment sequence through alternative attempts, contributing directly to the mis-pull.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: Magland's more technical and visually demanding environment — shorter, more positive terrain with civilization directly below — combined with Philippe's limited wingsuit BASE experience at this site likely increased cognitive demands during the deployment phase. The unfamiliar visual environment and terrain proximity may have saturated his capacity to execute the BOC reach while managing flight and altitude awareness.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Jonathan had only 1 BASE season and 160 BASE jumps. The narrative explicitly states he 'would have benefited from more experience before attempting this flight to inform the intuition needed for making such a quick judgement call off exit.' This exit required site-specific intuition for rapid ridge-crossover-or-deploy decisions that develops through accumulated wingsuit BASE experience at demanding terrain sites, which he had not yet built.

L2_preconditions.label: Adverse weather conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative reports medium winds and rain at the time of the jump. Rain and wet conditions can degrade tracking performance by affecting body position stability and suit aerodynamics. The jumper had been waiting two days of rain to jump, and the witnesses noted conditions were wet, which may have contributed to degraded tracking execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Repeated gainer exits with wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'was used to jumping a gainer with his wingsuit' in the previous days, indicating a pattern of performing aerials from this exit. Aerials with wingsuits are explicitly identified as exponentially dangerous due to bridle entanglement risk, loss of altitude awareness, and instability. The repeated practice of this high-risk maneuver without apparent corrective action after normalizing it represents a failure to correct a known deficiency — the inherent control recovery challenge of combining aerials with wingsuit flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perceive speed loss
L2_preconditions.description: The FlySight data shows constant and progressive speed loss from the first moment, yet the narrative states Nacho 'should not have noticed' this degradation and continued the complex line. His failure to perceive the deteriorating airspeed — the critical cue that should have triggered an abort — directly enabled the decision to continue the complex line rather than redirecting to the simpler channel exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical overexertion from approach
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly identifies fatigue from the 4-hour, 1300m+ winter alpine approach with full climbing equipment (ice axe, crampons, harness, ropes) through abundant snow in poor conditions as an intrinsic factor. This physical exertion likely degraded cognitive and physical performance, contributing to reduced situational awareness and the failure to recognize the poor start and progressive speed loss that should have prompted an abort.

L2_preconditions.label: Katabatic backwind degraded flight performance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative identifies strong backwind (katabatic airflow) as a factor that caused descent and further height loss on the recovery arc. All three jumpers who jumped that day had poor start numbers, attributed to the backwind. This environmental condition mechanically degraded the wingsuit's flight performance by reducing effective airspeed and glide, directly contributing to the progressive speed loss that made the complex line unviable and should have triggered the abort decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was most likely rendered unconscious upon striking the ledge approximately 30 meters below the exit after the sled struck the protruding rock. The witness observed the jumper's head resting against his chest with no effort to correct instability or deploy his parachute, indicating loss of consciousness that prevented any recovery action. While this incapacitation was a consequence of the exit failure rather than a pre-existing condition, it is the physiological state that made the L1 error fatal by eliminating any possibility of corrective action.

L2_preconditions.label: TARD from unfamiliar exit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper performed a TARD from a cliff exit he had not done a TARD from before, and the narrative notes the cliff was 'not a very friendly one for TARDs.' This unfamiliar and suboptimal deployment method from this specific exit created conditions that contributed to the immediate 180° off-heading opening, which the jumper then failed to correct.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste from self-imposed goal
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had set a goal of 365 BASE jumps in the year, had fallen behind, and was trying to catch up by doing multiple jumps per day. The narrative describes him appearing 'a little rushed' during preparation and 'nervous on exit,' exiting 'quietly and without a count.' This self-imposed pressure and resulting haste degraded his preparation and exit quality, contributing to the conditions that produced the off-heading and his failure to respond.

L2_preconditions.label: Geographically Lost
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Fabrice exited from a point ~100m northeast of the known 'classic' exit, and investigators could not determine whether he was trying to open a new exit or mistakenly believed he was at the classic exit. The possibility that he misidentified his position on the mountain directly explains why he jumped from a non-jumpable cliff profile without adequate assessment.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate exit point assessment
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'we suppose he didn't really measure the place' — Fabrice jumped from a cliff profile that post-accident measurements confirmed was non-jumpable, indicating he failed to adequately assess the terrain profile before committing to the exit. He had visited the area twice before without his rig but apparently did not verify the specific exit point's jumpability on this occasion.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of currency on opening exits
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Fabrice 'was not current on opening exits' — evaluating and opening new or unfamiliar exit points is a specific skill requiring recent practice. This lack of currency directly contributed to his failure to recognize that the cliff profile was non-jumpable before committing to the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Unfamiliar container not adequately prepared for
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was flying a new LD5 container he had never jumped before, which had a different BOC placement than his previous Zack rig. Accepted practice requires thorough familiarization with new equipment before use in demanding applications. The witness confirmed that the LD5's BOC was difficult to reach if body position wasn't 100% correct, and the jumper had not adequately prepared for this difference, directly enabling the failed PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper was 'not very current at the moment' because that year he had been focusing on a paragliding course rather than BASE jumping. Reduced currency would degrade the fine motor skills and muscle memory needed for a smooth PC extraction, particularly when adapting to unfamiliar equipment, directly contributing to the failed pull attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: After spending 7 seconds fighting line twists following a 360-degree twist on opening, the jumper cleared the twists but then made zero control inputs for the remaining 8-9 seconds before cliff impact. This complete absence of any corrective action despite having a flyable canopy and time available is consistent with a freeze response — a temporary inability to act following the stress of the line twist emergency and the realization of proximity to the wall.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Injury During Impact Sequence
L2_preconditions.description: While speculative, the narrative describes the jumper losing speed gradually during flight and pulling after a degraded flight. The 360-degree line twists and 7 seconds spent clearing them while the canopy flew toward the wall may have resulted in a body position or physical state (e.g., disorientation, exhaustion from fighting twists) that impaired his ability to execute inputs in the remaining seconds. However, this is insufficiently supported to code with confidence.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Derrick had only 12 skydives, never graduated from AFF, and was banned from his DZ. The accepted minimum before beginning BASE is 200-250 skydives. This profound lack of foundational training left him without the knowledge and judgment framework needed to evaluate object suitability.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: Derrick had no mentor, no first jump course, and taught himself BASE jumping entirely on his own. He had 'huge gaps in his theoretical knowledge and skills that he seemed oblivious to,' including inability to assess object suitability. He took a packing class only one week before death and lied about his skydiving experience to the instructors.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 12 skydives and no AFF graduation, Derrick lacked the foundational freefall and deployment skills that skydiving training develops. On a 195ft antenna requiring precise BOC deployment timing with minimal margin, this absence of basic deployment proficiency directly contributed to the late extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: Without formal BASE training or mentorship, Derrick had not developed proper deployment technique for packed BOC deployments from low objects. He had exclusively performed unpacked rollover jumps and only took a packing class one week prior. The narrative indicates he did not know how to pack a parachute, suggesting this may have been among his first packed BOC deployments.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Despite 12 skydives, no AFF graduation, no BASE training, being banned from his DZ, multiple mishaps including a cliff strike one month prior, and repeated warnings from skydivers, BASE jumpers, and community members online, Derrick continued jumping increasingly dangerous objects solo. He told his AFF instructor he was 'fully aware of the risks,' demonstrating a persistent and unreasonable overestimation of his ability that far exceeded what any reasonable jumper would exhibit.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Derrick had been repeatedly warned by multiple community members, AFF instructors, and online contacts that he needed to stop BASE jumping and get proper training. He had experienced multiple mishaps including a cliff strike at Bixby Bridge one month before death and a dangerous low turn under canopy at the DZ. Despite awareness of these deficiencies through direct feedback and personal near-misses, he continued jumping progressively more dangerous objects without corrective action.

L2_preconditions.label: Sinking air in shaded terrain
L2_preconditions.description: The grass section of the mountain was still shaded at the time of the jump, creating cold, sinky air conditions on that side of the ridge. This atmospheric condition directly degraded the jumper's glide performance and available lift as he came around the corkscrew maneuver, contributing to his inability to maintain adequate separation from terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of Currency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Marta had not jumped her wingsuit for about three months due to an ankle injury. While she made some wingsuit skydives the week before, a three-month gap in wingsuit BASE jumping represents a significant currency deficit for a discipline where precise motor patterns degrade without regular practice. This reduced currency directly relates to the head-low exit and failure to recover stable flight, a skill-based error in execution.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known mis-pull issue
L2_preconditions.description: James experienced a mis-pull the day before the fatal jump and discussed it with peers, acknowledging the need to return to skydiving to perfect the deployment technique associated with his new flare method. Despite this awareness of a known deficiency, he continued competing without correcting the problem, and the same failure mode recurred fatally.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Assess Wind Before Exit
L2_preconditions.description: The narrator states that Logan appeared to be 'already in go mode' when positioning on his exit platform, 'exiting without gauging what the wind was doing in the moment.' This indicates a failure to maintain situational awareness of the immediate wind conditions at the critical moment of exit, directly contributing to his choice of exit position and timing.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient WS BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 1 prior wingsuit BASE jump. Wingsuit BASE accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump, with many subsequent jumps needed to develop proficiency. With only 2 BASE seasons and 1 prior WS BASE jump, the jumper lacked the experience to manage wingsuit flight near terrain, directly contributing to the loss of control and stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient exit consistency for technical object
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Busto's exits were known to be inconsistent, and on at least two occasions earlier in the season he was recommended to remain at Brento to work on exits rather than participate in other jumps. The notes further state he was 'jumping beyond personal capability' at a technical exit where 'a poor launch was simply not survivable.' This established pattern of insufficient exit proficiency directly contributed to the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known exit deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Busto was aware of his inconsistent exits through direct feedback from peers who recommended he stay at Brento to work on exits on at least two occasions earlier in the season. Despite this recurring feedback, he proceeded to a technical cliff exit requiring consistent performance rather than continuing remedial practice at a more forgiving object.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's intent was to film his buddy during the two-way wingsuit flight. The possible factors explicitly list 'Focusing on filming' and 'Altitude awareness,' indicating that attention directed toward the filming task diverted focus from altitude monitoring and timely deployment preparation, contributing to the failure to locate the pilot chute.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded spatial awareness at night
L2_preconditions.description: The jump occurred well after sunset in dark conditions. Reduced visibility would have impaired Sergey's ability to perceive his proximity to the cliff face and assess his separation during freefall and deployment, directly degrading his ability to execute adequate forward separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Redemption motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Sergey had experienced a serious accident at High Ultimate in 2023 involving aerials and a 180 off-heading resulting in severe injuries. He had expressed a strong desire to return for 'redemption,' and reports indicate he was progressively getting into 'sendy mode' during the trip. This motivation plausibly drove his decision to attempt a late solo night jump from the same object where he had previously been seriously injured performing aerials.

L2_preconditions.label: Below-average tracking skills
L2_preconditions.description: Sergey's tracking skills were documented as below average, with a very shallow, almost positive angle of attack resulting in poor forward separation performance. This skill deficiency directly contributed to his inability to achieve adequate object clearance on a slider-up cliff that requires effective tracking for safe separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Night conditions degrading visual reference
L2_preconditions.description: The jump took place well after sunset with limited natural light. Even with a 75% moon, conditions were quite dark. These conditions directly impaired Sergey's ability to maintain orientation, judge terrain proximity, and assess his separation from the cliff during freefall and under canopy, as evidenced by toggles remaining stowed suggesting he never achieved stable canopy flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention on Flyby
L2_preconditions.description: Eric formulated a last-minute flyby plan while zipping up his wingsuit at the exit point, and during flight he directed his attention toward executing the flyby (turning 15-20 degrees right, narrating instructions to his partner). This channelized attention on the flyby task likely contributed to him initiating his flare and deployment reach later and lower than he otherwise would have, leaving him in a degraded position (low altitude, post-flare deceleration) when he needed to execute the PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate Flyby Planning
L2_preconditions.description: The flyby was formulated last-minute at the exit point while Eric was zipping up his wingsuit, with no specific coordination on height, location, or communication technology. This inadequate planning resulted in Eric flying the flyby at approximately 150ft above terrain over the last ledge of the talus, leaving critically insufficient altitude and time for deployment when he subsequently struggled with PC extraction.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The investigation conclusion explicitly states 'Lack of knowledge in complex static line jumping (I don't know what I don't know).' While the jumper had 300 BASE jumps and 3 seasons, the narrative confirms the inverted static line technique was 'still new to him.' Accepted practice requires specific instruction and supervised progression for unpacked/unconventional deployments; the jumper's knowledge gap regarding the specific rigging, body position, and entanglement risks of an inverted static line jump directly enabled the decision to attempt it.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate rigging and exit setup
L2_preconditions.description: The updated investigation identifies multiple preparation failures: the anchor point was positioned on the handrail vertical cable rather than the stepping surface, which was not ideal for the exit position and introduced asymmetric loading risk. Additionally, big gloves were hanging from his wrists, creating a snag/entanglement hazard during a deployment where bridle routing through the legs was required. These configuration choices created the physical conditions enabling the bridle/PC entanglement with his leg that produced the total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste
L2_preconditions.description: The investigation conclusion explicitly identifies 'nervousness and in a rush' and 'external influence' as contributing factors. Being in a rush at the exit point — particularly for a complex, unfamiliar deployment method — is directly contrary to accepted practice, which emphasizes taking time at the exit point and not rushing checklists. This haste likely degraded the quality of his pre-jump verification and exit execution for a technique requiring meticulous preparation.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states he felt 'really good (too good)' at the exit and was 'overconfident and willing to try this line immediately.' His partner and multiple community members had warned him repeatedly over years about his approach, but he dismissed all concerns. This overconfidence drove his decision to fly an aggressive proximity line on his first jump from this exit rather than following the conservative briefing.

L2_preconditions.label: Visual illusion from leafless trees
L2_preconditions.description: The updated narrative describes a specific visual illusion: leafless winter trees are nearly invisible from above, causing the jumper to perceive greater clearance than actually existed. This perceptual degradation directly contributed to his failure to see the dry tree he struck, enabling the aggressive line choice to produce the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that a similar incident with an unseen dry tree had happened to him just weeks prior ('this happened to him already few weeks back but he got away without anything'), and that multiple people had spoken to him 'thousand times over the years' about his jumping behavior. He had many close calls throughout his BASE career yet consistently failed to correct his approach, representing a sustained pattern of ignoring known recurring risks.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states he had 'almost no wingsuit skydiving experience' and 'essentially did his first wingsuit jumps there [at Brento].' The domain reference requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives before first wingsuit BASE jump. This severe training deficiency meant he lacked the foundational skills in flight efficiency, speed management, and glide assessment needed to recognize the hazards of the aggressive proximity line he chose.

L2_preconditions.label: Snowy/wet exit surface
L2_preconditions.description: The exit point was covered in snow in winter conditions. Despite the jumper's efforts to clear and dry the rock, the surrounding terrain remained snow-covered and the rock surface had been wetted. The snowy/icy surface directly degraded the jumper's ability to maintain footing during the exit push, causing the slip.
